question: What does DHCP stand for?
choice_a: Dynamic Host Control Protocol
choice_b: Dynamic Host Configuration Protocol
choice_c: Distributed Host Connectivity Protocol
choice_d: Direct Host Configuration Process
explanation: DHCP = Dynamic Host Configuration Protocol — it automatically assigns IP address; subnet mask; gateway; and DNS to clients.
aspect_key: automatic client ip configuration (address; mask; gateway; dns)
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/NETC-121/Notes List C - Personal Notes/Lecture Week 4/DHCP and DORA.md

question: What does the letter D stand for in the DORA process?
choice_a: Deliver
choice_b: Discover
choice_c: Distribute
choice_d: Designate
explanation: DORA = Discover; Offer; Request; Acknowledge — the four steps of the DHCP address negotiation.
aspect_key: discover; offer; request; acknowledge
source_path: /home/citadel/Documents/NETC-121/Notes List C - Personal Notes/Lecture Week 4/DHCP and DORA.md

question: What does the letter O stand for in the DORA DHCP process?
choice_a: Offer
choice_b: Open
choice_c: Organize
choice_d: Outbound
explanation: DORA = Discover; Offer; Request; Acknowledge. "O" = Offer — the DHCP server responds to the Discover with an IP address offer.
aspect_key: discover; offer; request; acknowledge

question: What does the letter R stand for in the DORA DHCP process?
choice_a: Reject
choice_b: Relay
choice_c: Request
choice_d: Route
explanation: DORA = Discover; Offer; Request; Acknowledge. "R" = Request — the client formally requests the offered IP from the DHCP server.
aspect_key: discover; offer; request; acknowledge

question: What does the letter A stand for in the DORA DHCP process?
choice_a: Assign
choice_b: Authorize
choice_c: Authenticate
choice_d: Acknowledge
explanation: DORA = Discover; Offer; Request; Acknowledge. "A" = Acknowledge — the DHCP server confirms the lease assignment to the client.
aspect_key: discover; offer; request; acknowledge

question: What four pieces of information does DHCP automatically provide to a client?
choice_a: IP address; username; password; and gateway
choice_b: IP address; subnet mask; default gateway; and DNS server address
choice_c: IP address; MAC address; hostname; and VLAN ID
choice_d: Subnet mask; domain name; proxy settings; and firewall rules
explanation: DHCP provides: (1) IP address; (2) subnet mask; (3) default gateway IP; and (4) DNS server IP — the four essential items a host needs to communicate on an IP network.
aspect_key: automatic client ip configuration (address; mask; gateway; dns)

question: What is a static IP address?
choice_a: An IP address that is automatically assigned by a DHCP server from a pool
choice_b: An IP address manually configured on a device and fixed — it does not change unless an administrator changes it
choice_c: An IP address that expires after a set lease time
choice_d: An IP address that changes every time a device reconnects to the network
explanation: A static IP is manually set by an administrator and remains constant. Used for servers; printers; and network devices that need a predictable address.
aspect_key: pooled assignment vs manually fixed settings

question: What is a dynamic IP address?
choice_a: An IP address that is permanently burned into the NIC hardware
choice_b: An IP address that is automatically assigned from a DHCP pool for a limited lease period
choice_c: An IP address that changes every packet transmission
choice_d: An IP address reserved for routers and cannot be assigned to client devices
explanation: A dynamic IP is leased from a DHCP server for a set period. When the lease expires the address may be renewed or returned to the pool for reassignment to another device.
aspect_key: pooled assignment vs manually fixed settings

question: What does DNS stand for?
choice_a: Dynamic Naming System
choice_b: Domain Name Server
choice_c: Domain Name System
choice_d: Distributed Network Service
explanation: DNS = Domain Name System — the hierarchical distributed system that translates human-readable hostnames (e.g. www.example.com) into IP addresses.
aspect_key: name-to-ip resolution at internet and enterprise scale

question: What is the primary function of DNS in a network?
choice_a: To encrypt all web traffic between client and server
choice_b: To translate domain names like www.example.com into IP addresses that routers can use
choice_c: To assign IP addresses to client devices automatically
choice_d: To provide authentication for network login requests
explanation: DNS resolves hostnames to IP addresses. Without DNS users would need to memorize IP addresses for every website and service they visit.
aspect_key: name-to-ip resolution at internet and enterprise scale

question: What does FQDN stand for?
choice_a: Forwarded Query Domain Name
choice_b: Fully Qualified Domain Name
choice_c: Full Query DNS Node
choice_d: Functional Qualified Domain Notation
explanation: FQDN = Fully Qualified Domain Name — the complete hostname plus domain that uniquely identifies a host on the internet (e.g. mail.example.com).
aspect_key: host label + domain hierarchy identifies exact target

question: In the FQDN mail.example.com what portion is the hostname?
choice_a: com
choice_b: .example.com
choice_c: mail
choice_d: example
explanation: In an FQDN the leftmost label is the hostname: "mail" identifies the specific host. "example.com" is the domain.
aspect_key: host label + domain hierarchy identifies exact target

question: What is a TLD in DNS?
choice_a: A Top-Level Domain — the rightmost label in a domain name such as .com; .org; or .gov
choice_b: A Total Link Descriptor used in BGP routing tables
choice_c: A Type-Length Data record in a DNS response packet
choice_d: A Transit Layer Designator used for MPLS tagging
explanation: TLD = Top-Level Domain — the rightmost portion of a domain name. Examples: .com (commercial); .org (organization); .gov (US government); .uk (country code).
aspect_key: root; tld; domain; host/subdomain levels

question: Which level sits at the very top of the DNS hierarchy above all TLDs?
choice_a: The ISP's DNS server
choice_b: The local enterprise DNS server
choice_c: The DNS root zone
choice_d: The DHCP server
explanation: The DNS root zone is the top of the hierarchy. Root servers know the authoritative name servers for every TLD. There are 13 root server IP addresses (a.root-servers.net through m.root-servers.net).
aspect_key: root; tld; domain; host/subdomain levels

question: What does a DNS recursive resolver do when it receives a name query from a client?
choice_a: It immediately returns an APIPA address if the name cannot be resolved
choice_b: It walks the DNS hierarchy — querying root then TLD then authoritative name server — until it obtains the IP address and returns it to the client
choice_c: It checks only its local cache and fails if the entry is not there
choice_d: It forwards all queries directly to the ISP's mail server
explanation: A recursive resolver does the heavy lifting for the client — it queries root; then TLD; then the authoritative server step by step until the answer is found. The client only sees the final result.
aspect_key: recursive resolution; authoritative responses; and caching

question: What is a DHCP scope?
choice_a: The physical area covered by the DHCP server's wireless signal
choice_b: The configured range of IP addresses that a DHCP server is authorized to assign on a specific subnet
choice_c: The maximum number of clients that can connect to a DHCP server simultaneously
choice_d: The list of denied MAC addresses excluded from DHCP service
explanation: A DHCP scope defines the pool of IP addresses; subnet mask; lease duration; and options (gateway; DNS) configured for a specific subnet. The server assigns addresses only from this defined range.
aspect_key: per-subnet allocation boundaries and options

question: What is a DHCP reservation?
choice_a: A configuration that blocks a client from receiving a DHCP address
choice_b: A DHCP server setting that always assigns the same IP address to a specific device based on its MAC address
choice_c: A temporary hold on an IP address pending administrator approval
choice_d: A fallback IP assigned when the DHCP pool is exhausted
explanation: A DHCP reservation ties a specific IP address to a device's MAC address — the DHCP server always gives that device the same IP ensuring consistent addressing without requiring a static configuration on the device itself.
aspect_key: fixed ip lease tied to device mac

question: What is a DHCP lease?
choice_a: The agreement between a router and an ISP for IP address allocation
choice_b: The time period for which a DHCP server grants an IP address to a client — after expiry the client must renew or the address returns to the pool
choice_c: The physical network port assignment for each DHCP client
choice_d: The VLAN tag assigned with each DHCP IP address
explanation: A DHCP lease is temporary by design. Default lease times vary by vendor (e.g. 24 hours; 8 days). When 50% of the lease elapses the client attempts renewal; if not renewed the IP returns to the pool.
aspect_key: duration controls churn and address reuse behavior

question: What is WINS and what did it provide?
choice_a: WINS is a wireless intrusion network sensor
choice_b: WINS (Windows Internet Name Service) was a legacy Microsoft service that resolved NetBIOS computer names to IP addresses before modern DNS was widely adopted
choice_c: WINS is a protocol for wireless network IP assignment replacing DHCP
choice_d: WINS is the Windows implementation of DNS used on all modern Windows networks
explanation: WINS resolved NetBIOS names — the flat computer names (like "SERVER1") used by older Windows networks — to IP addresses. It was superseded by DNS as the standard naming system.
aspect_key: legacy microsoft name service preceding modern dns usage

question: What is network latency?
choice_a: The rate at which data is transferred across a network link
choice_b: The time delay between sending a piece of data and it being received at the destination; measured in milliseconds
choice_c: The number of packets lost during transmission
choice_d: The physical distance a signal must travel measured in kilometers
explanation: Latency is the time delay for data to travel from source to destination. Causes include propagation delay (distance/speed of light); processing delay at routers; and queuing delay at congested nodes.
aspect_key: time delay metric measured in milliseconds

question: What is jitter in the context of network communications?
choice_a: The maximum bandwidth available on a network connection
choice_b: The variation in packet arrival delay over time — inconsistent intervals between packets arriving at the destination
choice_c: The number of times a packet is retransmitted before being dropped
choice_d: The signal strength fluctuation on a wireless network
explanation: Jitter measures inconsistency in delivery timing. If packet 1 arrives 10ms after sending and packet 2 arrives 30ms after sending the jitter is 20ms. High jitter disrupts real-time applications like VoIP because audio arrives in uneven bursts.
aspect_key: packet delay variation affecting real-time traffic quality

question: What type of communication allows data to flow in only one direction?
choice_a: Simplex
choice_b: Half-duplex
choice_c: Full-duplex
choice_d: Multicast
explanation: Simplex communication is one-directional only — data flows from sender to receiver with no return channel. Example: broadcast radio or television transmission.
aspect_key: simplex; half-duplex; full-duplex communication behavior

question: What type of communication allows data to flow in both directions but only one direction at a time?
choice_a: Simplex
choice_b: Half-duplex
choice_c: Full-duplex
choice_d: Broadcast
explanation: Half-duplex allows bidirectional communication but only one direction at a time — like a walkie-talkie where you press to talk and release to listen. Ethernet hubs operated in half-duplex mode.
aspect_key: simplex; half-duplex; full-duplex communication behavior

question: What type of communication allows data to flow in both directions simultaneously?
choice_a: Simplex
choice_b: Half-duplex
choice_c: Full-duplex
choice_d: Anycast
explanation: Full-duplex allows simultaneous bidirectional communication. Modern Ethernet switch-to-device connections use full-duplex — both devices can send and receive at the same time.
aspect_key: simplex; half-duplex; full-duplex communication behavior

question: What is RS-232 used for in networking?
choice_a: Encrypting management traffic on enterprise routers
choice_b: Serial communication over a console cable to access and configure network devices via a terminal session
choice_c: Providing high-speed backbone connections between core switches
choice_d: Assigning IP addresses to devices through a serial management interface
explanation: RS-232 is a serial communication standard used for console access to network equipment. A console cable (DB-9 or RJ-45 rollover) connects a computer's serial port to the device's console port for out-of-band management.
aspect_key: start/stop/parity/data bits; baud rate; signal levels

question: What is multiplexing in networking?
choice_a: The process of encrypting multiple data streams simultaneously
choice_b: Combining multiple data streams onto a single shared communication channel
choice_c: Splitting a single packet into multiple smaller fragments
choice_d: Assigning multiple IP addresses to a single NIC
explanation: Multiplexing allows multiple independent data streams to share one physical transmission medium efficiently — without it each conversation would need a dedicated physical link.
aspect_key: multiple data streams share one medium

question: What does FDM stand for in multiplexing?
choice_a: Frequency Division Multiplexing
choice_b: Frame Division Method
choice_c: Full Duplex Modulation
choice_d: Forwarding Decision Matrix
explanation: FDM = Frequency Division Multiplexing — each data stream is assigned a different frequency band on the shared channel; all streams transmit simultaneously on their own frequency.
aspect_key: split by frequency bands vs split by time slots

question: What does TDM stand for in multiplexing?
choice_a: Total Duplex Modulation
choice_b: Terminal Data Management
choice_c: Time Division Multiplexing
choice_d: Traffic Data Metric
explanation: TDM = Time Division Multiplexing — the shared channel is divided into repeating time slots; each data stream gets assigned specific slots and transmits only during its allocated time.
aspect_key: split by frequency bands vs split by time slots

question: What does the baud rate setting in an RS-232 serial connection control?
choice_a: The encryption strength of the management session
choice_b: The number of signal changes per second which determines the data transmission speed of the serial link
choice_c: The number of parity bits added to each byte
choice_d: The timeout period before the console session disconnects automatically
explanation: Baud rate is the number of signal state changes per second on the serial line. Common console baud rate: 9600 baud. Both devices must be configured to the same baud rate or the terminal session will produce garbled output.
aspect_key: start/stop/parity/data bits; baud rate; signal levels

question: What is Wireshark?
choice_a: A network design tool used to create logical network diagrams
choice_b: A free open-source packet capture and analysis tool that displays detailed contents of network traffic in real time
choice_c: A Cisco proprietary tool for configuring switch VLANs
choice_d: A wireless network scanner that identifies available SSIDs
explanation: Wireshark captures network packets and displays their full contents — source/destination MAC; IP addresses; ports; protocol type; and payload — making it an essential tool for troubleshooting and learning network protocols.
aspect_key: installation; capture; and protocol visibility for packet analysis

question: Which type of DNS server stores the original authoritative records for a domain and provides definitive answers?
choice_a: A recursive resolver
choice_b: A caching server
choice_c: An authoritative name server
choice_d: A DNS forwarder
explanation: An authoritative name server holds the official zone records for a domain — A records; MX records; CNAME records — and returns authoritative answers for names within its zone.
aspect_key: recursive resolution; authoritative responses; and caching

question: What is a DNS forwarder?
choice_a: A device that routes IP packets between DNS servers
choice_b: A DNS server configured to send queries it cannot resolve locally to an upstream resolver
choice_c: A hardware appliance that accelerates DNS responses
choice_d: A backup DNS server that takes over when the primary is unavailable
explanation: A DNS forwarder passes unresolved queries to another DNS server (typically the ISP's DNS or a public resolver like 8.8.8.8) rather than performing its own recursive resolution.
aspect_key: upstream resolver for unresolved/non-local queries

question: Why is high jitter particularly harmful for VoIP (voice over IP) calls but less harmful for file downloads?
choice_a: File downloads use UDP which is immune to jitter
choice_b: High jitter causes voice packets to arrive at uneven intervals — the audio is reconstructed with gaps or stutters; file downloads buffer data and are unaffected by delivery timing as long as the total bytes eventually arrive
choice_c: Jitter only affects wireless connections; VoIP uses wired connections
choice_d: VoIP calls are encrypted making them more sensitive to network variations
explanation: VoIP requires packets to arrive at consistent intervals matching the audio sampling rate. High jitter = irregular packet arrival = choppy audio or dropout. File downloads have no real-time requirement — the OS buffers received data and the application reads from the buffer when it's ready.
aspect_key: packet delay variation affecting real-time traffic quality

question: What advantage does full-duplex have over half-duplex for Ethernet connections between a switch and a computer?
choice_a: Full-duplex requires fewer cables than half-duplex
choice_b: Full-duplex doubles effective bandwidth by allowing simultaneous transmission in both directions eliminating the need for collision management
choice_c: Full-duplex is required only for speeds above 1 Gbps
choice_d: Full-duplex provides encryption that half-duplex does not
explanation: Full-duplex: NIC transmits and receives simultaneously — 1 Gbps full-duplex provides 1 Gbps in each direction. Half-duplex: the link is shared so effective throughput is less than the nominal speed due to collision overhead.
aspect_key: simplex; half-duplex; full-duplex communication behavior

question: In which real-world scenario is a DHCP reservation most useful?
choice_a: For guest devices that should get temporary addresses and automatically expire
choice_b: For servers and printers that need to be reachable at a consistent IP address but should receive address settings from DHCP rather than manual static configuration
choice_c: For devices that should be blocked from accessing the network
choice_d: For mobile devices that frequently move between subnets
explanation: DHCP reservation provides predictable addressing (same IP every time) without requiring manual static configuration on the device. Servers and printers benefit — they're always at the same address; other systems can reference them reliably.
aspect_key: fixed ip lease tied to device mac

question: How does FDM (Frequency Division Multiplexing) allow multiple conversations to share a single cable?
choice_a: FDM assigns time slots to each conversation so they take turns transmitting
choice_b: FDM assigns each conversation a different frequency band so all conversations transmit simultaneously on the same medium without interfering with each other
choice_c: FDM compresses conversations into smaller data packets that can share a cable
choice_d: FDM encrypts each conversation's data using a unique key before combining
explanation: FDM: each channel gets a dedicated frequency band. Think of cable TV — hundreds of channels all transmitted on the same cable at different frequencies. Your TV's tuner filters for just the frequency of the channel you want.
aspect_key: split by frequency bands vs split by time slots

question: How does TDM (Time Division Multiplexing) allow multiple conversations to share a single channel?
choice_a: TDM assigns each conversation a different frequency range
choice_b: TDM divides time into repeating slots and assigns each conversation specific slots — each stream transmits only in its allocated slots while the others wait
choice_c: TDM compresses data packets to allow multiple streams to transmit simultaneously
choice_d: TDM uses different cable pairs for each conversation within the same cable
explanation: TDM: the channel is sliced into time slots. Stream A transmits in slots 1; 4; 7; … Stream B in slots 2; 5; 8; … Each gets periodic access. Traditional T1 circuits use TDM with 24 channels of 64 Kbps each.
aspect_key: split by frequency bands vs split by time slots

question: What type of application is most negatively affected by high network latency?
choice_a: Bulk file transfers of large video files
choice_b: Automated nightly database backups
choice_c: Interactive real-time applications like online gaming; VoIP; and video conferencing
choice_d: Sending an email with a large attachment
explanation: High latency makes interactive applications feel slow or unresponsive — a 200ms latency means every keystroke or action has a perceptible delay. Batch transfers can tolerate high latency since they just need total throughput not quick responses.
aspect_key: time delay metric measured in milliseconds

question: What information in a DNS A record maps together?
choice_a: Domain name to MAC address
choice_b: Hostname to IPv4 address
choice_c: IPv4 address to hostname (reverse lookup)
choice_d: Domain name to mail server
explanation: A DNS A record maps a hostname (e.g. www.example.com) to its IPv4 address. When a browser resolves a URL it requests the A record for the hostname.
aspect_key: recursive resolution; authoritative responses; and caching

question: What RS-232 parameter specifies how many data bits are in each transmitted byte?
choice_a: Baud rate
choice_b: Parity
choice_c: Data bits
choice_d: Stop bits
explanation: RS-232 console settings include data bits (typically 8) which determines how many bits make up each character. The standard "8N1" setting means 8 data bits; No parity; 1 stop bit.
aspect_key: start/stop/parity/data bits; baud rate; signal levels

question: What does DHCP use to broadcast its Discover message to find a server?
choice_a: It unicasts to the known DHCP server IP address
choice_b: It sends the Discover to the subnet broadcast address 255.255.255.255 because the client has no IP and does not know the DHCP server's address
choice_c: It multicasts to all DHCP servers using a reserved multicast address
choice_d: It sends the Discover directly to the default gateway which relays it
explanation: DHCP Discover: client has no IP; destination IP = 255.255.255.255 (broadcast); source IP = 0.0.0.0. All devices on the segment receive the broadcast; only DHCP servers respond.
aspect_key: discover; offer; request; acknowledge

question: What is the difference between a DNS cache and an authoritative DNS server?
choice_a: DNS cache and authoritative servers are the same thing with different names
choice_b: A DNS cache stores previously resolved responses temporarily to speed up future queries; an authoritative server stores the official zone records and provides definitive answers for names in its domain
choice_c: A DNS cache only stores negative (NXDOMAIN) responses; authoritative servers store only positive answers
choice_d: DNS caches are only used on client computers; authoritative servers are only used by ISPs
explanation: Caching: a resolver caches answers from authoritative servers to serve future identical queries without repeating the full lookup. Cache entries have a TTL after which they expire and must be re-fetched. Authoritative: holds the master zone file; never expires records arbitrarily.
aspect_key: recursive resolution; authoritative responses; and caching

question: Which DHCP option carries the IP address of the device's default gateway?
choice_a: Option 1 (Subnet Mask)
choice_b: Option 3 (Router)
choice_c: Option 6 (Domain Name Servers)
choice_d: Option 15 (Domain Name)
explanation: DHCP Option 3 is the Router option — it provides the client's default gateway IP address. Option 1 = subnet mask; Option 6 = DNS server IPs; Option 15 = domain name.
aspect_key: per-subnet allocation boundaries and options

question: What happens when a DHCP lease expires and the client cannot reach the DHCP server to renew?
choice_a: The client permanently loses its IP and cannot communicate until manually configured
choice_b: The client continues using the expired address indefinitely since no other device claims it
choice_c: The client self-assigns an APIPA address (169.254.x.x) after failing to renew and can only communicate with other APIPA-addressed devices on the local segment
choice_d: The client broadcasts its old IP to the network to confirm it is still in use
explanation: Lease expiry without renewal: the client's OS detects expiry; attempts DHCPREQUEST and DHCPDISCOVER; if no server responds it falls back to APIPA (169.254.x.x) allowing only local-link communication.
aspect_key: duration controls churn and address reuse behavior

question: In which direction does DNS name resolution typically flow through the hierarchy?
choice_a: From the client up to root; down through TLD; to authoritative; back to client
choice_b: From the root down to the client directly without intermediate servers
choice_c: From the client directly to the authoritative server using the domain name
choice_d: From the ISP up to the root and then down to the client
explanation: DNS resolution: client → recursive resolver → root (who handles .com?) → TLD (.com servers: who handles example.com?) → authoritative server (here is the IP) → recursive resolver caches and returns answer to client.
aspect_key: root; tld; domain; host/subdomain levels

question: What is the purpose of the binary addressing foundation in subnetting?
choice_a: Binary addresses are only used in IPv6; IPv4 uses decimal addresses
choice_b: IP addresses are 32-bit binary numbers and subnetting decisions about network vs host boundaries are made by performing binary operations on those 32 bits; understanding binary is required to understand how subnet masks isolate the network portion of an address
choice_c: Binary is only used by routers; end-host computers use decimal for all networking
choice_d: Binary addressing only applies to Class A networks; Class B and C use decimal
explanation: IPv4 addresses are 32 binary bits. Subnet masks are also 32 binary bits with contiguous 1s marking network bits and 0s marking host bits. The bitwise AND of address and mask = network address. Subnetting cannot be understood without understanding binary bit values.
aspect_key: bit values define network/host boundaries in subnetting

question: What does Wireshark reveal that makes it useful for demonstrating the security difference between HTTP and HTTPS?
choice_a: Wireshark shows whether HTTPS loads pages faster than HTTP
choice_b: Wireshark captures HTTP traffic in plaintext showing usernames and passwords visibly; HTTPS traffic appears as encrypted ciphertext that cannot be read — demonstrating why HTTPS is essential for sensitive communications
choice_c: Wireshark measures the bandwidth used by HTTP vs HTTPS to show encryption overhead
choice_d: Wireshark shows the number of TCP connections opened by each protocol
explanation: HTTP vs HTTPS in Wireshark: HTTP packets show full request headers; cookies; and any form data (including credentials) in readable plaintext. HTTPS packets show only the TLS record type and encrypted ciphertext — the content is unreadable without the session keys. This is the most impactful demonstration of why HTTPS matters.
aspect_key: installation; capture; and protocol visibility for packet analysis

question: What is a DNS MX record used for?
choice_a: Mapping a hostname to an IPv4 address
choice_b: Specifying which mail servers are responsible for accepting email for a domain
choice_c: Providing an alternate name (alias) for an existing hostname
choice_d: Resolving an IPv4 address back to a hostname in reverse DNS
explanation: DNS MX (Mail Exchanger) records identify which mail servers accept incoming email for a domain. When you send email to user@example.com your mail server queries MX records for example.com to find where to deliver the message.
aspect_key: recursive resolution; authoritative responses; and caching

question: What does parity in RS-232 serial communication provide?
choice_a: Data compression for faster transmission
choice_b: A basic error detection mechanism that appends a bit to each character to enable detection of single-bit transmission errors
choice_c: Encryption of transmitted characters before they are sent
choice_d: The speed negotiation between sender and receiver
explanation: RS-232 parity: an extra bit added to each character. Even parity: total 1-bits in character+parity = even. Odd parity: total = odd. None (N): no parity bit. The receiver checks the parity bit to detect single-bit errors. Common console setting: 8N1 (8 data bits; No parity; 1 stop bit).
aspect_key: start/stop/parity/data bits; baud rate; signal levels

question: Where can the DHCP server role be hosted in a network?
choice_a: Only on a dedicated physical server separate from other network functions
choice_b: On a dedicated server; an enterprise router/firewall; or a network switch — a DHCP server role can be hosted by different device types depending on network size
choice_c: Only on the ISP's equipment — DHCP cannot be run internally
choice_d: Only on wireless access points for wireless clients
explanation: DHCP server location varies by deployment: home routers (router performs DHCP); small offices (firewall or router); enterprise (Windows Server or Linux DHCP server in a data center with DHCP relay agents on each subnet).
aspect_key: automatic client ip configuration (address; mask; gateway; dns)

question: Why must both the client's IP address and its subnet mask be included in a DHCP Offer rather than just the IP address?
choice_a: The subnet mask is optional and can be determined by the device from the IP address class alone
choice_b: Without the subnet mask the client cannot determine which addresses are on its local subnet — this affects whether traffic goes directly to the destination or through the default gateway; the mask and IP address together define the host's network location
choice_c: The subnet mask is always /24 for all DHCP assignments so it doesn't need to be explicitly provided
choice_d: The subnet mask is included only as a backup in case the client's static mask is wrong
explanation: IP + mask together define the network boundary. A client with 192.168.1.50 and /24 knows 192.168.1.0-255 is local. The same IP with /16 would think a much larger range is local and would not route correctly. DHCP Option 1 provides the mask alongside the IP in the Offer.
aspect_key: automatic client ip configuration (address; mask; gateway; dns)

question: What happens in a network environment where two DHCP servers are active and configured with overlapping address ranges?
choice_a: Both servers cooperate and split the address pool evenly between themselves
choice_b: Both servers may offer IP addresses from the same pool — if a client accepts both offers the same address could be assigned to two different clients causing an IP conflict; properly designed DHCP deployments use non-overlapping scopes or a failover protocol
choice_c: Clients automatically detect two DHCP servers and use the faster-responding one safely
choice_d: DHCP servers broadcast their pool ranges and automatically eliminate duplicate addresses
explanation: Rogue DHCP server problem: two servers may offer the same addresses causing IP address conflicts. DHCP snooping on switches mitigates this by only allowing DHCP Offer/Ack packets from trusted (uplink) ports — untrusted ports can send DHCP Discover but not Offer.
aspect_key: discover; offer; request; acknowledge

question: Why is DNS resolution typically faster for repeated queries to the same hostname than for first-time queries?
choice_a: DNS uses faster TCP connections for repeated queries and slower UDP for first-time queries
choice_b: Recursive resolvers cache responses for the duration of the record's TTL — subsequent clients querying the same hostname receive the cached answer instantly without repeating the full root→TLD→authoritative lookup chain
choice_c: DNS servers prioritize known hostnames by moving them to faster hardware
choice_d: Repeated queries use a compressed DNS format that reduces packet size
explanation: DNS caching: the recursive resolver stores each response with its TTL. Within the TTL all clients asking the same question get the cached answer (typically <1ms). First query: 5-50ms walking the hierarchy. Cache hit: ~1ms. This is why DNS changes (IP address changes) take time to propagate globally — caches hold old answers until their TTL expires.
aspect_key: recursive resolution; authoritative responses; and caching

question: How does a DHCP relay agent allow a single DHCP server to serve multiple subnets even though DHCP Discover messages use broadcast?
choice_a: A DHCP relay agent converts DHCP broadcasts to multicast so they cross router boundaries
choice_b: A DHCP relay agent (IP helper) intercepts the DHCP broadcast on each subnet; unicasts it to the centralized DHCP server; and relays the DHCP Offer back to the client — this allows one DHCP server to serve all subnets without placing a separate server on each
choice_c: A DHCP relay agent broadcasts the Discover on all subnets simultaneously
choice_d: The relay agent requires the DHCP server to be on the same physical switch as each client
explanation: Relay agent operation: router interface configured as relay/helper captures DHCP broadcast; adds its own IP as the relay agent address (Option 82); unicasts the packet to the DHCP server IP. The server uses the relay IP to determine which scope to assign from. Without relay: one DHCP server per subnet required.
aspect_key: per-subnet allocation boundaries and options

question: What is the specific operational difference between a DHCP scope exclusion and a DHCP reservation?
choice_a: Both exclusions and reservations prevent addresses from being assigned automatically
choice_b: A DHCP exclusion removes specific addresses from the pool so they are never assigned dynamically — leaving them free for manual static assignment; a DHCP reservation assigns a specific address permanently to a specific MAC but the address is managed by DHCP; both serve different administrative needs
choice_c: Exclusions block specific MAC addresses from receiving any DHCP address
choice_d: Reservations require the device to send a special reservation request code in the Discover packet
explanation: Exclusion: remove 192.168.1.1-20 from the DHCP pool → these stay unassigned (for static-configured routers; switches; servers). Reservation: MAC 00:11:22:33:44:55 always gets 192.168.1.100 from DHCP. An exclusion frees addresses for manual use; a reservation manages an address through DHCP for a specific device.
aspect_key: fixed ip lease tied to device mac

question: Why does shortening the DHCP lease duration increase the load on the DHCP server while lengthening it creates different problems?
choice_a: Shorter leases reduce DHCP server load by giving out fewer addresses
choice_b: Shorter leases cause clients to renew more frequently increasing DHCP server transaction volume; longer leases reduce renewal frequency but cause address exhaustion when many devices connect briefly (hotel Wi-Fi; conference rooms) because departed clients' addresses are not released until lease expiry
choice_c: Longer leases increase server load because the server must track more long-term reservations
choice_d: Lease duration has no effect on DHCP server load — all leases are processed identically
explanation: Lease tuning: short leases (1 hour) — high-churn environments (airports; hotspots) where many devices connect temporarily; long leases (8 days) — stable enterprise where devices rarely change. A short lease in a stable environment wastes server cycles. A long lease in a high-churn environment exhausts the pool — departed devices' addresses are unavailable until the lease expires.
aspect_key: duration controls churn and address reuse behavior

question: What is the specific role of the DNS root zone and why are there only 13 root server IP addresses?
choice_a: The DNS root zone is the authoritative record holder for all domain names globally
choice_b: The root zone contains NS (Name Server) records pointing to the authoritative TLD servers (the .com servers; .org servers; .gov servers) — it does not contain records for specific domains; 13 addresses is a historical UDP constraint (512-byte limit); each address is served by clusters of anycast servers globally numbering in the hundreds
choice_c: The root zone stores all DNS records to respond to every DNS query directly
choice_d: 13 root server IPs are required by the ITU as a security measure
explanation: Root server function: responds to "who is authoritative for .com?" not "what is the IP of www.google.com?". Root servers don't know final answers — they know TLD servers. 13 IP addresses (A-M) fit in a 512-byte UDP DNS response. Each IP is served by many physical servers in different countries via anycast routing.
aspect_key: root; tld; domain; host/subdomain levels

question: How does network latency compound when multiple hops are involved in a path across the internet and what does this imply for application design?
choice_a: Latency is constant across all hops because routers compensate for delay
choice_b: Each router hop adds its own propagation; queuing; and processing delay — total RTT = sum of all per-hop delays; applications communicating across 10 hops with 10ms average delay per hop experience 100ms RTT minimum; applications sensitive to latency must either minimize hop count (CDNs; edge computing) or design around latency (asynchronous operations; buffering)
choice_c: Routers reduce latency by caching packets so only the first hop has full delay
choice_d: Latency only affects UDP applications; TCP has built-in latency compensation
explanation: Latency accumulation: each router processes the packet (typically 0.1-1ms) plus propagation delay (distance/speed). Cross-continental: 60-100ms. Transatlantic: 80-150ms. CDN (Content Delivery Network) strategy: place servers close to users to minimize hops and distance. Database applications that make hundreds of sequential queries are extremely sensitive to latency per query.
aspect_key: time delay metric measured in milliseconds

question: Why do VoIP applications implement a jitter buffer and what trade-off does increasing the buffer size create?
choice_a: Jitter buffers encrypt VoIP packets to prevent interception
choice_b: A jitter buffer holds received packets for a short time to smooth out delivery timing — inconsistently arriving packets are delayed in the buffer until their "slot" arrives for playback; increasing the buffer size accommodates more jitter but adds fixed latency which itself degrades call quality; there is an optimal balance between jitter tolerance and added latency
choice_c: Jitter buffers retransmit dropped packets automatically filling gaps in audio
choice_d: Jitter buffers convert UDP packets to TCP for reliable VoIP delivery
explanation: Jitter buffer: receiver holds packets for N milliseconds before playing — this allows late-arriving packets to catch up. 20ms buffer: tolerates jitter up to 20ms but adds 20ms fixed delay. 200ms buffer: tolerates high jitter but the call feels like satellite phone delay. Optimal jitter buffer size = just enough to smooth jitter without adding perceptible latency (typically 20-80ms).
aspect_key: packet delay variation affecting real-time traffic quality

question: What is the difference between a DNS A record; CNAME record; and PTR record and when is each used?
choice_a: All three DNS record types store hostname-to-IP mappings but at different protocol layers
choice_b: A record maps hostname to IPv4 address; CNAME (Canonical Name) creates an alias pointing one hostname to another hostname (following the chain to find the final A record); PTR record maps an IP address back to a hostname for reverse DNS lookup — each serves different resolution scenarios
choice_c: CNAME records provide faster resolution than A records; PTR records are only used by routers
choice_d: A records and CNAME records are identical — the CNAME is only used as a backup when the A record fails
explanation: Record types: A (forward: hostname → IPv4). CNAME (alias: www.example.com → example.com → then look up A record for example.com). PTR (reverse: 1.168.192.in-addr.arpa → hostname; used by spam filters and security tools to verify sending IP matches claimed hostname).
aspect_key: recursive resolution; authoritative responses; and caching

question: What is the relationship between an FQDN and a DNS zone and why does adding a trailing dot to an FQDN matter?
choice_a: An FQDN and a DNS zone are the same thing
choice_b: An FQDN (mail.example.com) identifies one specific host; a DNS zone (example.com) is the administrative unit containing all records for a domain; the trailing dot (mail.example.com.) represents the DNS root and makes the name absolute — without the dot some software treats the name as relative and may append the local domain suffix causing incorrect resolution
choice_c: The trailing dot encrypts the DNS query to prevent spoofing
choice_d: FQDN and DNS zone both refer to TLD records only
explanation: Absolute vs relative FQDN: "mail.example.com" without a dot could be interpreted by the resolver as "mail.example.com.localdomain" if search domains are configured. "mail.example.com." (with trailing dot = root) is absolute — no search domain appended. DNS zone files always use trailing dots for FQDNs to prevent ambiguity.
aspect_key: host label + domain hierarchy identifies exact target

question: How does the DHCP DORA process use broadcast messages and why does the client use the server's offered IP without explicit acknowledgement for a brief window?
choice_a: After the Offer the client immediately starts using the offered IP without waiting for Acknowledge
choice_b: After the Discover; the client receives Offers from potentially multiple DHCP servers; it selects one and sends a Request (broadcast — informing all servers it chose one); only after receiving the Acknowledge does the client officially configure and use the IP; the Request is broadcast so rejected servers can reclaim their tentative offers
choice_c: DHCP clients always send Requests as unicast after the Offer to reduce broadcast traffic
choice_d: DHCP clients send the Acknowledge to the server; not the server to the client
explanation: DORA broadcast pattern: Discover = broadcast (no IP yet). Offer = unicast from server to client's MAC. Request = broadcast (client says "I choose server X" — all servers see this and rejected servers release their tentative offers). Acknowledge = unicast from server confirming lease. Client configures IP only after receiving ACK.
aspect_key: discover; offer; request; acknowledge

question: What is a duplex mismatch and what symptoms does it cause on an Ethernet link?
choice_a: A duplex mismatch occurs when one device has a faster CPU than the other
choice_b: A duplex mismatch occurs when one end of a link is configured full-duplex and the other is half-duplex — the full-duplex end transmits while the half-duplex end is transmitting causing collisions; the half-duplex end detects collisions and backs off but the full-duplex end has no collision detection creating high error rates; late collisions; and severely degraded throughput
choice_c: A duplex mismatch is when two devices have different IP addresses on the same subnet
choice_d: A duplex mismatch only occurs on wireless links where devices cannot negotiate speed
explanation: Duplex mismatch symptom: high CRC/FCS error count; late collision counters incrementing on the half-duplex side; throughput well below expected (often 10-40% of nominal speed). Cause: autonegotiation failure (one side forced; other side autonegotiating). Fix: set both ends to the same explicit duplex setting or enable autonegotiation on both.
aspect_key: simplex; half-duplex; full-duplex communication behavior

question: Why does Wireshark show HTTP login credentials in plaintext but cannot show HTTPS credentials even with full packet capture access?
choice_a: HTTPS uses a different TCP port so Wireshark cannot decode port 443 traffic
choice_b: HTTP sends all data including form POST bodies (usernames; passwords) unencrypted in the TCP payload — Wireshark reads the TCP payload directly; HTTPS wraps all application data in TLS which encrypts the payload before TCP encapsulation — Wireshark sees the TLS record but the content is ciphertext without the session keys
choice_c: Wireshark can show HTTPS credentials but only for enterprise certificate-pinned applications
choice_d: HTTPS credentials are stored at the OS level not in network packets so Wireshark has no access
explanation: Wireshark HTTP: the TCP payload contains the raw HTTP request — headers; form data; cookies — all visible. HTTPS: TLS encrypts the application data before handing it to TCP. Wireshark sees "TLSv1.3 Application Data" with a length but the bytes are ciphertext. To decrypt HTTPS in Wireshark you need the session key log file (the private key alone is insufficient for modern TLS with forward secrecy).
aspect_key: installation; capture; and protocol visibility for packet analysis

question: What specific security risk does running Telnet demonstrate in Wireshark and how does this differ from SSH?
choice_a: Wireshark captures Telnet traffic but it appears encrypted because Telnet uses its own encryption
choice_b: Wireshark captures Telnet sessions in complete plaintext — every character typed including passwords appears as individual bytes in the TCP stream; Wireshark's ""Follow TCP Stream"" function reassembles the full session making credentials instantly readable; SSH encrypts all session data including authentication meaning the stream shows only encrypted bytes
choice_c: Telnet uses UDP so Wireshark cannot capture it
choice_d: SSH and Telnet appear identical in Wireshark because both use port 22
explanation: Telnet in Wireshark: Follow TCP Stream shows the complete session — login prompt; typed username; typed password (character by character); all commands entered; all output returned. Nothing is hidden. This is the definitive demonstration of why Telnet is prohibited on modern networks. SSH in Wireshark: encrypted payload — credentials and commands are opaque.
aspect_key: installation; capture; and protocol visibility for packet analysis

question: How does the DNS TTL (Time to Live) affect how quickly a DNS record change propagates to all internet users?
choice_a: DNS TTL is a quality setting with no practical effect on propagation speed
choice_b: TTL tells caching resolvers how long to keep a record — if a record has TTL 86400 (24 hours) resolvers cache it for 24 hours; changing the IP address at the authoritative server is instant but resolvers with cached old answers continue using the old IP until their TTL expires; reducing TTL before a planned IP change allows faster propagation but increases DNS query load on the authoritative server
choice_c: DNS record changes propagate instantly because resolvers always check the authoritative server
choice_d: TTL controls the encryption lifetime not the caching duration
explanation: Propagation mechanics: change A record IP at registrar/authoritative server → takes effect immediately for new queries. But existing resolvers' caches hold the old answer until TTL expires. Best practice before planned changes: reduce TTL to 300 seconds (5 min) 24 hours before the change — caches expire quickly after the change. Restore high TTL afterward.
aspect_key: recursive resolution; authoritative responses; and caching

question: Why is WINS considered a legacy system and what specifically made it unsuitable for modern enterprise networks?
choice_a: WINS was replaced because it uses UDP while modern networks require TCP
choice_b: WINS used a flat NetBIOS namespace with no hierarchy — it could not scale beyond a single domain; required replication between WINS servers across sites; and was incompatible with the internet's DNS-based addressing; modern Active Directory domains use DNS for all name resolution and WINS is only maintained for backward compatibility with legacy applications
choice_c: WINS was insecure because it transmitted passwords in plaintext unlike DNS
choice_d: WINS required dedicated hardware that became too expensive to maintain
explanation: WINS limitations: flat namespace (no subdomain hierarchy); per-site replication complexity; no internet integration; no standard cross-platform support. DNS replaced it with a hierarchical scalable distributed system that works identically on Windows; Linux; and across the internet. Windows Server still supports WINS for legacy NetBIOS applications.
aspect_key: legacy microsoft name service preceding modern dns usage

question: What is the specific RS-232 parameter that controls error synchronization and why does 8N1 remain the universal default for console access?
choice_a: 8N1 is only used for fiber optic console connections; copper console uses 7E1
choice_b: 8N1 (8 data bits; No parity; 1 stop bit) was standardized as the default because it transfers 8-bit bytes (one complete ASCII or binary character per frame) with no overhead parity bit (parity adds minimal value at modern error rates) and one stop bit — 8N1 is the highest-throughput common setting and became the default on all major network vendors' console ports
choice_c: 8N1 uses parity bits for error detection making it more reliable than 7E1
choice_d: The parity setting in RS-232 must be set to "Even" for Cisco devices and "Odd" for Juniper devices
explanation: 8N1 encoding: start bit (1) + 8 data bits (8) + no parity (0) + stop bit (1) = 11 signal events per character. 7E1: 7 data bits + even parity bit + 1 stop bit. 8N1 is dominant because: 8-bit bytes match all modern character encodings; no parity overhead; 1 stop bit for efficiency. 9600 8N1 is the universal default for all major vendor console ports (Cisco; Juniper; Aruba).
aspect_key: start/stop/parity/data bits; baud rate; signal levels

question: Why does multiplexing make communication networks economically viable where dedicated per-connection circuits would not be?
choice_a: Multiplexing compresses data making it smaller and thus cheaper to transmit
choice_b: Without multiplexing each telephone call or internet connection would require a dedicated physical wire from end-to-end across the entire network — the cost of provisioning separate physical circuits for every possible communicating pair would make nationwide or global communication economically impossible; multiplexing lets one physical circuit carry thousands of simultaneous conversations
choice_c: Multiplexing is only used for wireless networks; wired networks use dedicated circuits
choice_d: Multiplexing was invented to improve security by separating traffic on shared cables
explanation: Economic necessity: a country with 100 million phone subscribers would need 5×10^15 possible dedicated circuits for every pair to communicate directly. Multiplexing allows one physical fiber to carry 10;000+ simultaneous conversations via WDM (Wavelength Division Multiplexing). The entire internet is built on shared multiplexed infrastructure.
aspect_key: multiple data streams share one medium

question: What is the practical difference between FDM and TDM in terms of what happens to one stream when the other stream has nothing to transmit?
choice_a: Both FDM and TDM automatically reallocate unused capacity to active streams
choice_b: In FDM each stream has a permanently dedicated frequency band — if stream A has nothing to transmit its frequency band goes unused even though other streams might benefit from that capacity; in TDM unused time slots are wasted unless statistical TDM (statistical multiplexing) is used; IP packet switching uses statistical multiplexing where capacity is only consumed by streams that are actually transmitting
choice_c: FDM reallocates frequency bands dynamically every millisecond to active streams
choice_d: TDM eliminates wasted capacity by compressing inactive streams' data
explanation: Efficiency comparison: FDM — traditional radio/cable TV. Each channel uses its frequency always. Silence = wasted capacity. TDM — traditional telephony. Silent phone = wasted time slot. Statistical multiplexing (modern IP networks) — bandwidth is shared dynamically; idle streams consume zero bandwidth; active streams burst up to full link capacity. Statistical multiplexing is why shared internet works efficiently.
aspect_key: split by frequency bands vs split by time slots

question: Why must all DHCP addresses assigned to a subnet come from the same network range as the subnet and what happens if they do not?
choice_a: DHCP can assign any IP address from any scope regardless of subnet
choice_b: If the DHCP server assigns an address from a different subnet range the client configures itself with an IP address in a different network than the connected switch port — the client cannot communicate because its IP doesn't match the local broadcast domain; ARP requests and routing will fail; proper scope configuration matches the IP range to the physical subnet
choice_c: Addresses from other subnets still work because DHCP handles the routing automatically
choice_d: DHCP automatically corrects mismatched subnet assignments during the Acknowledge phase
explanation: Subnet matching requirement: if a host in 192.168.1.0/24 receives 10.0.0.50/8 from DHCP it configures 10.0.0.50. It tries to ARP for local hosts using 10.x.x.x addresses — but the local subnet is 192.168.1.x. No ARP replies. Default gateway is on 192.168.1.0/24 — also unreachable. The host is completely isolated despite having a valid IP.
aspect_key: per-subnet allocation boundaries and options

question: How does DNS prevent a single point of failure in name resolution and what mechanism allows this distributed resilience?
choice_a: DNS has a single central server that all devices query causing a single point of failure
choice_b: DNS resilience comes from multiple mechanisms: multiple root servers worldwide (anycast); multiple TLD servers for each TLD; authoritative zones can have primary and secondary servers; recursive resolvers are deployed redundantly; all servers cache results reducing load and providing answers even during partial outages
choice_c: DNS uses RAID storage to prevent data loss but still has a single point of failure at the server level
choice_d: DNS only has single servers at each level but uses TCP for reliable connections preventing failure
explanation: DNS redundancy: 13 root IP addresses (hundreds of actual physical servers via anycast). Each TLD has multiple authoritative servers. Zones can specify multiple NS records — both primary and secondary authoritative servers. Recursive resolvers cache responses — even if the authoritative server is temporarily unreachable cached answers continue serving clients.
aspect_key: root; tld; domain; host/subdomain levels

question: What is the purpose of the DHCP server's ping check before offering an IP address and why might an administrator disable this feature?
choice_a: The ping check encrypts the offered IP address before sending the Offer
choice_b: Before offering an IP address some DHCP servers ping that IP to verify it is unoccupied — if the address responds another device already has it statically configured; the server skips to the next address; administrators may disable this to speed up DHCP response time on large networks where ping checks add significant delay
choice_c: The ping check is required by RFC and cannot be disabled
choice_d: The ping check validates the client's MAC address rather than the offered IP
explanation: DHCP conflict detection: Windows DHCP Server sends 2 ICMP pings before offering an address. If any reply: conflict detected; address marked as BAD; next address tried. This prevents assigning IPs already in use by statically configured devices. Cost: adds 2+ seconds per address offer. Disabled in high-performance environments where address conflicts are managed administratively.
aspect_key: per-subnet allocation boundaries and options

question: Why does a network engineer configure specific exclusions in a DHCP scope rather than simply making the scope smaller?
choice_a: Making the scope smaller would block addresses from being dynamically assigned entirely
choice_b: Exclusions allow non-contiguous addresses within the scope range to be reserved for statically configured devices — for example scope 192.168.1.1-254 with exclusions 192.168.1.1-20 (routers; switches; servers) means DHCP assigns 192.168.1.21-254 dynamically; the excluded range is free for manual static assignment keeping the entire subnet logically organized
choice_c: Exclusions encrypt specific addresses preventing DHCP from logging those assignments
choice_d: Making the scope smaller would cause the excluded addresses to be unroutable
explanation: Scope design pattern: define the full subnet range in the scope; exclude the static-assignment block. Advantages: all addresses are visually managed in one scope; the static range is documented by the exclusion; future scope changes don't leave static addresses orphaned. Alternative: split scope (DHCP only gets .100-.254; .1-.99 reserved for statics) — simpler but static range can't be centrally documented.
aspect_key: per-subnet allocation boundaries and options

question: What is the binary value of the decimal number 192 and why is this specific pattern important in subnetting?
choice_a: 192 in binary is 10101010 which appears as the most significant bits of a Class C address
choice_b: 192 in binary is 11000000 — two consecutive 1-bits set to 1 starting from the most significant bit; this pattern appears in the /26 subnet mask (11000000 in the last octet = 192) creating four equal /26 subnets from one /24 network; understanding that 192 = 11000000 is essential for manual subnet mask calculations
choice_c: 192 in binary is 11110000 representing a /28 subnet mask
choice_d: 192 is identical to 255 in binary because both indicate maximum values
explanation: Binary ↔ decimal: 128+64 = 192. Binary: 11000000. /26 mask last octet = 11000000 = 192. So 255.255.255.192 is the /26 mask. Subnets: 0; 64; 128; 192 (increments of 64 because the third significant bit = 2^6 = 64). Binary literacy is the foundation of all manual subnetting calculations.
aspect_key: bit values define network/host boundaries in subnetting

question: What does the DNS SOA (Start of Authority) record define and why is it critical for zone administration?
choice_a: SOA records define which devices in the network can query the DNS server
choice_b: The SOA record specifies the primary authoritative name server for the zone; the email of the zone administrator; the zone's serial number (incremented on every change); and timing parameters for secondary server refresh/retry/expiry — without a properly configured SOA secondary DNS servers cannot replicate the zone or determine when the zone data is stale
choice_c: SOA records provide the default gateway for all hosts in the DNS zone
choice_d: SOA records encrypt zone data to prevent unauthorized zone transfers
explanation: SOA fields: primary NS (who is master); admin email; serial number (secondaries compare to detect changes); refresh (how often secondaries poll); retry (if refresh fails); expire (max time secondaries serve zone without master contact); minimum TTL. Serial number must increment on every zone change or secondaries won't replicate the update.
aspect_key: recursive resolution; authoritative responses; and caching

question: What is the difference between static IP addressing's administrative burden and DHCP's operational simplicity at enterprise scale?
choice_a: Static IP has less administrative burden because it never requires renewal
choice_b: At enterprise scale managing hundreds or thousands of static IP assignments means tracking each device's IP in a spreadsheet; manually updating each device on moves/adds/changes; and coordinating with all teams; DHCP centralizes address management — adding/moving/reconfiguring a device requires no manual IP configuration; the DHCP server handles all assignments; changes to DNS or gateway settings can be pushed to all clients by updating scope options
choice_c: DHCP creates more administrative burden than static IP because DHCP servers require daily maintenance
choice_d: Static and DHCP have equivalent administrative burdens at enterprise scale
explanation: Scale argument: 500 static endpoints = 500 manual configurations. Device moves require update at the device AND update in any documentation. DHCP: add a device; it configures itself. Change the default gateway? Update one DHCP scope option; all clients get the new gateway on next renewal. Move a device to another subnet? DHCP assigns the correct addresses automatically.
aspect_key: pooled assignment vs manually fixed settings

question: How does WINS resolve NetBIOS names and what is the operational difference from DNS name resolution?
choice_a: WINS uses UDP port 53 — the same as DNS — making them interchangeable
choice_b: WINS resolves NetBIOS names (flat 15-character computer names like "FILESERVER") using a registration and query model — devices register their NetBIOS name with the WINS server at boot; clients query WINS for a name and receive the IP; DNS uses a hierarchical structured namespace (FQDN) with distributed authoritative servers — WINS is centralized; flat; and Windows-only while DNS is hierarchical; distributed; and universal
choice_c: WINS uses multicast while DNS uses unicast for name queries
choice_d: WINS and DNS use identical resolution mechanisms; WINS is just older software
explanation: WINS vs DNS: WINS = flat namespace ("SERVER01" — no domain hierarchy); centralized server; registration-based. DNS = hierarchical (server01.example.com); distributed authoritative zones; standard across all operating systems. Active Directory eliminated WINS by using DNS for all services — WINS still runs only for applications that use NetBIOS APIs.
aspect_key: legacy microsoft name service preceding modern dns usage

question: What happens to network performance when the Wireshark capture interface is set to promiscuous mode on a busy switch port?
choice_a: Promiscuous mode reduces network performance by encrypting all captured packets
choice_b: In promiscuous mode the NIC passes all frames on the wire to the OS regardless of destination MAC — on a switched network most frames are unicast to other devices so promiscuous mode shows minimal extra traffic; on a hub or SPAN port promiscuous mode captures all traffic; the CPU overhead of processing additional frames in software can impact performance on high-speed links
choice_c: Promiscuous mode prevents other devices from sending traffic while a capture is running
choice_d: Promiscuous mode changes the NIC to full-duplex causing a duplex mismatch
explanation: Promiscuous mode on switch: normally switches send only traffic destined for the connected device. Promiscuous mode lets the NIC accept ALL frames its port receives — but switches only send addressed or flooded frames. To see others' traffic: configure a SPAN/mirror port. CPU impact: copying every frame to the Wireshark software buffer; large captures exhaust memory/CPU.
aspect_key: installation; capture; and protocol visibility for packet analysis

question: What is the operational distinction between an iterative DNS query and a recursive DNS query and which type does a client typically send?
choice_a: Clients always send iterative queries; servers always send recursive queries
choice_b: In a recursive query the client asks its resolver to fully resolve the name — the resolver does all the work and returns a final answer; in an iterative query the server returns the best answer it has (often a referral to another server) and the client must follow up itself; clients send recursive queries to their resolver (doing no work); resolvers send iterative queries to root/TLD/authoritative servers
choice_c: Clients can only send one type of query and it is determined by the operating system brand
choice_d: Iterative queries use TCP; recursive queries use UDP
explanation: Typical DNS flow: Client → resolver: recursive (client says "get me the full answer"). Resolver → root: iterative (root says "ask the .com servers"). Resolver → .com: iterative (.com says "ask ns1.example.com"). Resolver → ns1.example.com: iterative (returns IP). Resolver → client: final answer. Clients do no work; resolvers do all work.
aspect_key: recursive resolution; authoritative responses; and caching

question: How does the binary value of the last octet of an IP address determine the subnet it belongs to when a /26 mask is used?
choice_a: The last octet always determines the host number regardless of subnet mask
choice_b: With a /26 mask the last octet's two most significant bits (bits 7-6 = values 128 and 64) are network bits — the four possible /26 subnets are .0 (bits 00); .64 (bits 01); .128 (bits 10); .192 (bits 11); the last 6 bits are the host portion; an address with last octet = 100 (binary 01100100) belongs to the .64 subnet because bits 7-6 = 01
choice_c: All last-octet values above 128 belong to one subnet with /26 mask
choice_d: The /26 mask only creates two subnets divided at octet value 128
explanation: /26 subnet analysis: mask = 255.255.255.192 = 11111111.11111111.11111111.11000000. Last octet: top 2 bits are network. 192.168.1.100: last octet = 100 = 01100100. Top 2 bits = 01 = subnet .64. This address is in 192.168.1.64/26 with host bits = 100100 = 36 → host 36.
aspect_key: bit values define network/host boundaries in subnetting

question: What is the purpose of a DNS forwarder in an enterprise network where the internal DNS server handles internal domain resolution?
choice_a: A DNS forwarder replaces the internal DNS server when it is overloaded
choice_b: An internal DNS server resolves internal hostnames (server01.corp.example.com) from its own zones — for external internet names (google.com; github.com) it cannot resolve these locally; a DNS forwarder configuration sends these external queries to a trusted upstream resolver (ISP DNS; 8.8.8.8; or a corporate internet proxy DNS) rather than performing its own root-walk recursion
choice_c: DNS forwarders are only used when the primary DNS server fails
choice_d: A DNS forwarder receives queries from the internet and forwards them to internal servers
explanation: Enterprise DNS forwarder pattern: internal DNS server answers corp.example.com from local zone. External query for google.com: forwarded to 8.8.8.8 (or ISP DNS). Benefits: internal queries stay internal; external queries use a trusted resolver; security policy can control which external resolver is used. Without forwarder: internal DNS must query root servers directly (requires outbound UDP/TCP 53 to internet).
aspect_key: upstream resolver for unresolved/non-local queries

question: What is the specific advantage of using a static IP for a server over DHCP and what is the risk if a server does use DHCP without a reservation?
choice_a: Static IP for servers allows faster network transmission speeds
choice_b: With a static IP the server's address never changes — other systems that reference it by IP can rely on stable connectivity; with DHCP and no reservation the server could receive a different IP after a lease expiry or DHCP server rebuild causing all systems pointing to the old IP to fail until updated
choice_c: DHCP provides faster network speeds for servers so static IP is only used in emergencies
choice_d: Servers with DHCP cannot communicate on IPv6 networks
explanation: Server stability requirement: 100 client computers have "\\192.168.1.50" mapped as a file server share. If the server's DHCP lease isn't renewed and it gets 192.168.1.75 instead — all 100 clients fail to connect. Static IP or DHCP reservation prevents this. DNS names (server.corp.example.com) add another abstraction layer but the DNS record still needs the correct IP.
aspect_key: pooled assignment vs manually fixed settings

question: What is the typical default DHCP lease time in Windows Server and why is this duration chosen as a reasonable default?
choice_a: Windows Server DHCP defaults to 1 hour because address pools are small
choice_b: Windows Server DHCP defaults to 8 days — this duration balances address pool conservation (addresses aren't held indefinitely by disconnected devices) with renewal overhead (clients renew at 50% of lease time = every 4 days; lower frequency means less DHCP traffic) — suitable for stable corporate environments where devices change infrequently
choice_c: The default lease time is set by the client's operating system not the server
choice_d: Default lease time is 30 days to minimize address churn on large networks
explanation: 8-day default rationale: corporate endpoints are stable — they're on daily; rarely move. 4-day renewal frequency is manageable for the DHCP server. 8 days also allows a device to survive a DHCP server outage for 8 days before losing its lease. Contrast: public Wi-Fi typically uses 1-4 hour leases because devices connect briefly and leave.
aspect_key: duration controls churn and address reuse behavior

question: How does Wireshark's display filter differ from its capture filter and when is each more appropriate?
choice_a: Display filters remove packets from the capture file permanently while capture filters keep all packets
choice_b: A capture filter is applied before capture begins and selects which packets the NIC passes to Wireshark — unselected packets are never recorded saving disk space; a display filter is applied after capture to the already-captured data — it hides packets without removing them allowing the analyst to toggle visibility; capture filters are used for targeted high-traffic captures and display filters for analysis of saved captures
choice_c: Capture filters use BPF syntax; display filters use a completely different language that is incompatible
choice_d: Display filters are applied at the hardware level before any software processing
explanation: Wireshark filter types: capture filter (libpcap BPF syntax: "port 80"): applied by the kernel to the NIC — only matching packets enter Wireshark. Display filter (Wireshark filter syntax: "http.request.method == GET"): applied to already-captured data in memory. Capture filter: useful when capturing high-traffic links (prevents disk exhaustion). Display filter: useful when analyzing broad captures to focus on specific traffic.
aspect_key: installation; capture; and protocol visibility for packet analysis

question: What specific information in the DHCP Acknowledge message finalizes the client's IP configuration and what does the client do immediately after receiving it?
choice_a: The Acknowledge message only confirms the IP — the client must separately request the subnet mask and gateway
choice_b: The DHCP Acknowledge includes the assigned IP address; subnet mask; lease duration; default gateway (Option 3); and DNS server IPs (Option 6) — upon receiving the ACK the client applies all these parameters to the network interface; optionally performs a gratuitous ARP to detect address conflicts; and begins communicating on the network
choice_c: The ACK only sets the IP address; gateway and DNS must be fetched via separate DHCP Option requests
choice_d: The client ignores the Acknowledge and uses the IP from the Offer message instead
explanation: DHCP ACK contents: yiaddr field (Your IP address); subnet mask; lease time; Option 3 (gateway); Option 6 (DNS); optional: Option 12 (hostname); Option 15 (domain name); Option 44 (WINS). Client action post-ACK: configure NIC with all received settings; send gratuitous ARP (DHCP conflict detection); begin normal network operation.
aspect_key: discover; offer; request; acknowledge

question: What is the practical network troubleshooting difference between measuring round-trip latency with ping versus one-way latency and why can asymmetric paths complicate diagnosis?
choice_a: Ping RTT is always exactly twice the one-way latency because paths are always symmetric
choice_b: Ping reports round-trip time (RTT) — the total time for a packet to reach the destination and the ICMP reply to return; if the forward and return paths take different routes (asymmetric routing) the RTT splits unevenly between directions; high RTT might be caused by 5ms forward path and 90ms return path — ping cannot reveal this asymmetry; one-way measurement tools (OWAMP) or tracert in both directions from each endpoint are needed to diagnose
choice_c: One-way latency is always exactly half the RTT measurement
choice_d: Asymmetric paths only occur on wireless networks
explanation: Asymmetric routing is common: forward path A→B through ISP X; return path B→A through ISP Y. If ISP Y has congestion the RTT appears high but the issue is on the return path. Tracert from A shows only forward path hops. Tracert from B would show the actual congested return path. This is why "ping is high" investigation requires probing from both ends.
aspect_key: time delay metric measured in milliseconds

question: What is the DHCP lease renewal process at the T1 and T2 timers and what is the difference between how renewal is attempted at each point?
choice_a: T1 is when the lease fully expires and T2 is a warning 50% before expiry
choice_b: At T1 (50% of lease time) the client sends a unicast DHCPREQUEST directly to the leasing server to renew — if the server responds with an ACK the lease is extended from that point; if T1 renewal fails and the lease reaches T2 (87.5% of lease time) the client broadcasts a DHCPREQUEST to any available DHCP server to rebind; if rebind also fails the lease expires and the client falls back to APIPA
choice_c: The client always waits for the lease to fully expire before attempting renewal
choice_d: T1 and T2 are both broadcast-based renewal attempts sent to all servers simultaneously
explanation: DHCP renewal timeline: T1 = 50% of lease (unicast to original server — "please renew my lease"). T2 = 87.5% of lease (broadcast to any server — "I'm desperate; any server take me"). Expiry = 100% — client loses IP; self-assigns 169.254.x.x. This two-stage design gives the original server first chance to renew; only escalating to a broadcast if it is unreachable.
aspect_key: duration controls churn and address reuse behavior

question: What is the purpose of RS-232 start and stop bits and why does the receiver need them if it already knows the baud rate?
choice_a: Start and stop bits carry the actual data payload of each character frame
choice_b: RS-232 is asynchronous — sender and receiver share a baud rate but have no shared clock signal; the start bit (a single low bit) signals the receiver that a character is beginning and synchronizes its sampling clock; the stop bit (one or two high bits) signals the end of the character and returns the line to idle — without start/stop bits the receiver cannot determine where one character ends and the next begins
choice_c: Start and stop bits encrypt the character to prevent eavesdropping on the serial line
choice_d: The receiver counts bits from the moment it is powered on to find character boundaries
explanation: Asynchronous serial: the line sits at idle (logic 1 = mark). Start bit (logic 0 = space) wakes the receiver and triggers its bit-sampling timer. 8 data bits follow sampled at baud-rate intervals. Stop bit (logic 1) confirms end and returns line to idle. RS-232 does not need a clock wire because start/stop bits provide per-character synchronization.
aspect_key: start/stop/parity/data bits; baud rate; signal levels

question: What RS-232 voltage levels represent logic 1 and logic 0 and how do these differ from the TTL logic levels used inside computers?
choice_a: RS-232 uses 0V for logic 0 and 5V for logic 1 — the same as TTL
choice_b: RS-232 uses negative voltage (−3V to −15V) to represent logic 1 (mark) and positive voltage (+3V to +15V) to represent logic 0 (space) — the inverted polarity and higher voltages compared to TTL (0V = 0; 3.3V/5V = 1) improve noise immunity over longer cable runs; a USB-to-serial adapter contains a level converter chip that translates between RS-232 voltages and USB/TTL levels
choice_c: RS-232 uses 12V for logic 1 and 0V for logic 0 — the same polarity as TTL but at higher voltage
choice_d: RS-232 and TTL use identical voltage levels; the only difference is baud rate
explanation: RS-232 voltage: +3V to +15V = logic 0 (space). −3V to −15V = logic 1 (mark). Note polarity inversion from TTL. Standard ±12V on DB-9 pins. Noise immunity: larger voltage swing withstands cable-induced noise over runs up to 15m at 9600 baud. USB-to-serial adapters contain MAX232 or similar chips to perform the voltage translation.
aspect_key: start/stop/parity/data bits; baud rate; signal levels

question: What is statistical multiplexing and how does it differ from fixed TDM in terms of bandwidth efficiency for bursty data traffic?
choice_a: Statistical multiplexing is simply another name for frequency division multiplexing
choice_b: Statistical multiplexing dynamically allocates channel capacity only to streams that are actively transmitting — a stream with nothing to send consumes no bandwidth; fixed TDM assigns slots to every stream regardless of activity so a silent stream wastes its allocated slots; statistical multiplexing is far more efficient for bursty IP traffic where devices transmit in short bursts with idle gaps between
choice_c: Statistical multiplexing uses fixed slot sizes like TDM but randomizes which stream gets each slot
choice_d: TDM is more efficient than statistical multiplexing for internet traffic because it prevents collisions
explanation: Statistical multiplexing (packet switching): 10 streams share a 10 Mbps link. If 3 streams are active they can each burst up to ~3.3 Mbps. If only 1 stream is active it can use the full 10 Mbps. Fixed TDM: each stream permanently gets 1 Mbps regardless of activity. Internet packet switching = statistical multiplexing — idle hosts consume zero bandwidth; active hosts can burst.
aspect_key: multiple data streams share one medium

question: Why is jitter more disruptive to UDP-based real-time applications than TCP-based applications and what does this reveal about the design trade-off between UDP and TCP?
choice_a: TCP retransmits lost packets making it immune to all forms of network delay
