choice_b: TCP applications buffer received data and the OS reassembles the byte stream in order — jitter only affects delivery timing not final correctness; UDP applications receive packets in arrival order with no buffering or reordering by the transport layer — VoIP and video conferencing using UDP must play audio/video in real time so uneven arrival creates audible gaps or video stutter; the trade-off is that TCP's reliability mechanisms themselves add latency making TCP unsuitable for real-time media
choice_c: UDP is immune to jitter because it uses faster hardware paths than TCP
choice_d: Jitter only affects encrypted traffic; unencrypted UDP applications are immune
explanation: Design trade-off: TCP provides reliable ordered delivery but retransmission adds unpredictable delay — unacceptable for live voice. UDP is unreliable but delivers immediately — a jitter-affected packet played late is worse than a dropped one in VoIP. VoIP accepts occasional packet loss (brief silence) but cannot tolerate a late packet (it would delay all subsequent audio). This is why all real-time media protocols (RTP; QUIC media) use UDP.
aspect_key: packet delay variation affecting real-time traffic quality

question: What is the binary representation of the IPv4 loopback address 127.0.0.1 and what does it reveal about why the entire 127.0.0.0/8 block is reserved?
choice_a: Only the single address 127.0.0.1 is reserved; all other 127.x.x.x addresses are routable
choice_b: 127.0.0.1 in binary is 01111111.00000000.00000000.00000001 — the entire 127.0.0.0/8 block (the first octet = 01111111) is reserved by RFC 1122 for loopback; any packet sent to any 127.x.x.x address loops back to the sending host's own network stack without leaving the NIC; the /8 reservation wastes ~16 million addresses but was established during the early internet when address conservation was not a priority
choice_c: 127.0.0.1 in binary is 10000000.00000000.00000000.00000001 placing it in Class B address space
choice_d: Only 127.0.0.1 loops back; other 127.x.x.x addresses are routed normally to RFC 1918 private space
explanation: Loopback block: 127.0.0.0/8 first octet = 0111 1111. The IANA reserved the full /8 historically. In practice only 127.0.0.1 is commonly used though any 127.x.x.x address loops back. Binary value 0111 1111 = 64+32+16+8+4+2+1 = 127. Useful for testing the TCP/IP stack on a host without sending traffic to the physical network.
aspect_key: bit values define network/host boundaries in subnetting

question: What is a conditional DNS forwarder and how does it differ from a standard forwarder in a multi-domain enterprise environment?
choice_a: Conditional forwarders route all DNS queries to different servers depending on network speed
choice_b: A conditional forwarder routes queries to a specific DNS server based on the domain name being queried — queries for partner.company.com go to the partner company's DNS server while queries for external internet names go to the standard forwarder; a standard forwarder sends all unresolvable queries to one upstream server regardless of domain; conditional forwarding enables name resolution across corporate boundaries without merging DNS infrastructures
choice_c: Conditional forwarders only work for internal domains; external domains cannot be conditionally forwarded
choice_d: A conditional forwarder is the same as a DNS stub zone — both resolve names in external domains
explanation: Conditional forwarder use case: Company A (a.com DNS) acquires Company B (b.com DNS). To resolve b.com names without merging DNS: configure conditional forwarder on A's DNS — "for b.com; forward to 10.20.0.1 (B's DNS server)." All other external queries go to the standard forwarder. This avoids full DNS delegation and works for partner/merger/VPN scenarios.
aspect_key: upstream resolver for unresolved/non-local queries

question: How does DNS search domain configuration on a client affect how short hostnames (non-FQDNs) are resolved and what problem can this cause?
choice_a: DNS search domains prevent all non-FQDN queries from resolving
choice_b: When a user types a hostname without a domain (e.g. "fileserver") the OS appends each configured search domain in order and queries DNS for the result — "fileserver.corp.example.com" then "fileserver.example.com" until a match is found; this allows short names to work inside an enterprise; the problem is that a hostname matching a search domain suffix could accidentally resolve to an unintended external name if the internal DNS search is exhausted first
choice_c: DNS search domains are only applied to web browser queries not all applications
choice_d: Search domains only apply when the hostname contains a hyphen
explanation: Search domain resolution: client types "mail" → OS tries "mail.corp.example.com" (search domain 1) → if NXDOMAIN tries "mail.example.com" (search domain 2) → if still unresolved tries "mail" as a full name. Risk: if internal DNS does not have "mail.corp.example.com" the query may resolve to an external host at "mail.example.com" — potentially leaking the query externally. This is why FQDNs with trailing dots are used in scripts and config files.
aspect_key: host label + domain hierarchy identifies exact target

question: What is the difference between a network address and a broadcast address in a subnet and what does it mean that both are non-assignable to hosts?
choice_a: Network and broadcast addresses can be assigned to hosts as long as two devices do not share the same address
choice_b: In any subnet the address with all host bits set to 0 is the network address (identifies the subnet itself) and the address with all host bits set to 1 is the broadcast address (a packet sent here is delivered to every host in the subnet) — neither can be assigned to a host because routers use the network address for routing decisions and a host with the broadcast address would interfere with every broadcast packet; these two addresses reduce the usable host count by 2 from the total address space
choice_c: The broadcast address is assignable to hosts on modern networks because IPv6 replaced broadcast with multicast
choice_d: Only the network address is reserved; the broadcast address can be assigned on Cisco networks
explanation: Subnet example: 192.168.1.0/24. Network address = 192.168.1.0 (all host bits = 0). Broadcast = 192.168.1.255 (all host bits = 1). Usable hosts = 256 − 2 = 254. Binary: network = ...00000000; broadcast = ...11111111. This pattern applies universally regardless of subnet size — /30 has 4 addresses; 2 usable; /31 has 2 addresses; 0 usable (special point-to-point exception).
aspect_key: bit values define network/host boundaries in subnetting

question: How does Wireshark's Follow TCP Stream feature reconstruct application-layer data from individual packets and why is this more useful than reading individual packets?
choice_a: Follow TCP Stream only works for UDP streams; TCP streams must be analyzed packet by packet
choice_b: Wireshark's Follow TCP Stream reassembles the TCP byte stream by extracting payloads from all packets in a TCP session and concatenating them in sequence number order — this reconstructs the complete application-layer conversation (full HTTP request/response; Telnet session; FTP commands) exactly as the application saw it; individual packet analysis shows headers and fragments but the application data spans multiple packets making it unreadable without stream reassembly
choice_c: Follow TCP Stream is only available for encrypted HTTPS sessions
choice_d: Wireshark reconstructs TCP streams by replaying the capture in real time; it cannot work on saved files
explanation: Follow TCP Stream use: right-click any packet in a TCP session → Follow → TCP Stream → Wireshark reconstructs the entire bidirectional session. HTTP: shows full GET request; response headers; response body. Telnet: shows every character typed including passwords. This is the primary tool for demonstrating protocol security (or lack thereof) because it shows exactly what an attacker on the same network would see.
aspect_key: installation; capture; and protocol visibility for packet analysis

question: What is autonegotiation in Ethernet and what happens when only one side has autonegotiation enabled?
choice_a: Autonegotiation is only used for wireless Ethernet to negotiate channel width
choice_b: Autonegotiation is a protocol where both ends of an Ethernet link advertise their supported speeds and duplex modes and agree on the highest common capability — when only one side is set to autonegotiate and the other is forced to a fixed speed/duplex the autonegotiating side cannot detect the forced configuration and defaults to half-duplex (per IEEE 802.3 parallel detection rules) at the detected speed causing a duplex mismatch with the forced full-duplex side
choice_c: When one side uses autonegotiation and the other is forced; the autonegotiating side matches the forced side's settings
choice_d: Autonegotiation always chooses full-duplex regardless of what the remote end advertises
explanation: Autonegotiation parallel detection: if a device detects link pulses but no autonegotiation FLPs (Fast Link Pulses) from the remote end it applies the IEEE default — 10 or 100 Mbps at half-duplex. If the remote end is forced to 100 full-duplex: local side autonegotiates to 100 half-duplex → duplex mismatch → high CRC errors; poor throughput. Fix: either force both ends identically or enable autonegotiation on both.
aspect_key: simplex; half-duplex; full-duplex communication behavior

question: What is OFDM (Orthogonal Frequency Division Multiplexing) and how does it extend the FDM concept to improve resilience against frequency-selective interference?
choice_a: OFDM replaces FDM in all wired networks because it is always faster
choice_b: OFDM divides a channel into many narrow closely-spaced subcarrier frequencies that are mathematically orthogonal — each subcarrier carries a small portion of the data stream; subcarriers are spaced so their peaks align with the nulls of adjacent subcarriers eliminating inter-carrier interference; if one subcarrier frequency is impaired by interference only a small fraction of data is affected and forward error correction recovers it; this makes OFDM far more resilient to multipath interference than traditional single-carrier FDM
choice_c: OFDM uses time slots like TDM but labels them with frequency identifiers to improve speed
choice_d: OFDM is only used in satellite communications; Wi-Fi and cable networks use traditional FDM
explanation: OFDM applications: Wi-Fi (802.11a/g/n/ac/ax all use OFDM); LTE/5G; cable DOCSIS 3.1; DSL. Key advantage: frequency-selective fading (common in wireless multipath) degrades only specific subcarriers — the data on those subcarriers is recovered via FEC. Traditional single-carrier: a narrowband interferer affects the entire channel. OFDM = many narrow FDM subcarriers + mathematical orthogonality + FEC = highly efficient spectrum use.
aspect_key: split by frequency bands vs split by time slots

question: Why is the DHCP Discover sent to the broadcast address 255.255.255.255 rather than a subnet broadcast like 192.168.1.255 even when the client knows it is on a 192.168.1.x network?
choice_a: The client uses 192.168.1.255 if it already has an expired lease and 255.255.255.255 for first-time requests
choice_b: The DHCP client has no IP address and no subnet mask at the time it sends the Discover — because it has not yet received any configuration from DHCP it cannot calculate the subnet broadcast address; 255.255.255.255 is the limited broadcast address that is processed by all devices on the local segment regardless of IP configuration; using 255.255.255.255 requires no prior IP knowledge
choice_c: The client always sends the Discover to the subnet broadcast regardless of whether it has an IP
choice_d: 255.255.255.255 is used only when the DHCP server is on a different subnet
explanation: DHCP bootstrap problem: client needs an IP to communicate; but it needs to communicate to get an IP. Solution: 255.255.255.255 (limited broadcast; never forwarded by routers) works without IP knowledge. Source IP = 0.0.0.0 (client has no IP yet). All hosts on segment receive it; only DHCP servers respond. This is why 255.255.255.255 is also called the "all-hosts broadcast" for the local segment.
aspect_key: discover; offer; request; acknowledge

question: A network administrator notices that 50 client workstations have self-assigned 169.254.x.x addresses after a planned DHCP server maintenance window. The maintenance is over but clients still show 169.254.x.x. What is the most likely cause and correct remediation?
choice_a: The clients permanently lost their IP configuration and must be manually reconfigured
choice_b: After APIPA fallback clients continue attempting DHCP renewal on the T2 broadcast cycle but the DHCP server is back; the issue is likely that the server's scope lease database was not restored properly or the server service did not restart — remediation is to verify the DHCP service is running; confirm the scope is activated; then run ipconfig /release and ipconfig /renew on affected clients (or wait for their next T2 broadcast cycle)
choice_c: The 169.254.x.x addresses are permanent and require a full OS reinstall to clear
choice_d: APIPA addresses are only assigned during the maintenance window and clear automatically after exactly 1 hour
explanation: Diagnosis checklist: (1) verify DHCP service state — "net start DHCPServer" or services.msc. (2) Verify scope is active — Windows DHCP console shows scope status. (3) Verify address pool is not exhausted. (4) On a test client: ipconfig /release → ipconfig /renew → verify DHCP-assigned address. (5) If scope was deleted/corrupted during maintenance: recreate scope with same parameters and activate. Clients' next broadcast will succeed.
aspect_key: duration controls churn and address reuse behavior

question: A user reports intermittent connectivity where their VoIP calls are choppy but file downloads work fine. A packet capture shows consistent 20ms average latency but packet arrival intervals vary between 5ms and 60ms. What metric explains the problem and what is the recommended solution?
choice_a: The problem is high latency — the fix is to upgrade the WAN link to a lower-latency circuit
choice_b: The problem is high jitter (55ms variation between 5ms and 60ms arrival intervals) rather than high latency — average latency of 20ms is acceptable for VoIP; but the 55ms jitter exceeds the capacity of a standard 20ms jitter buffer causing packets to arrive after their playback deadline; the solution is to configure QoS to prioritize RTP/voice traffic and increase the VoIP client's jitter buffer size
choice_c: The problem is packet loss — jitter has no effect on VoIP if the latency is below 150ms
choice_d: The problem is a duplex mismatch on the client's NIC causing variable packet delivery timing
explanation: VoIP QoS remediation: (1) Enable DSCP marking — voice packets tagged EF (Expedited Forwarding; DSCP 46). (2) Configure queuing on routers/switches to prioritize EF-marked traffic (LLQ or CBWFQ). (3) Increase jitter buffer: 60ms would cover the 55ms worst-case jitter. (4) Verify no bandwidth saturation — congestion causes queue buildup → increased jitter. 20ms average latency with 55ms jitter = acceptable latency; unacceptable jitter.
aspect_key: packet delay variation affecting real-time traffic quality

question: A DNS record change was made 2 hours ago to point a web server hostname to a new IP address but some users still reach the old server while others reach the new one. No changes were made to the TTL before the update. What is the exact technical reason and how should this situation be resolved for future planned changes?
choice_a: DNS changes propagate instantly — the issue is a local hosts file override on affected machines
choice_b: The DNS record was changed at the authoritative server but resolvers that already cached the A record continue serving the old IP until their cached TTL expires; different users hit different resolvers with different cache ages — some already expired (seeing new IP) and some still have time remaining (serving old IP); the old TTL value (set before the change) controls how long the split-brain state persists; for future changes: reduce TTL to 300 seconds at least 24 hours before the planned IP change; let caches expire; make the change; then restore the higher TTL
choice_c: The split-brain state is caused by DNSSEC validation failures on some resolvers
choice_d: DNS propagation delay is a routing problem — the old server's routes are still active on half the internet
explanation: Split-brain DNS during migration: if old TTL was 86400 (24h) and the record was changed 2h ago — resolvers that cached 23h ago still have 21h remaining on their cached entry. Those resolvers serve old IP. Resolvers that queried after the change get new IP. Full propagation = up to 24h. Pre-change TTL reduction protocol: set TTL = 300 → wait 25h (old TTL+1h buffer) → change IP → all new cache entries expire in 5 min.
aspect_key: recursive resolution; authoritative responses; and caching

question: A network technician connects a laptop to a Cisco router's console port using a rollover cable and a USB-to-serial adapter. The terminal emulator shows garbled characters. The baud rate is set to 9600. What are the two most likely causes and what is the systematic approach to diagnosing them?
choice_a: The garbled output means the console cable is defective and must be replaced
choice_b: The two most likely causes are (1) incorrect serial parameters — the terminal emulator is set to a different combination of data bits; parity; or stop bits than the router's console port (Cisco default: 9600 8N1); and (2) the USB-to-serial adapter driver is not correctly installed or is presenting the wrong COM port; the systematic approach is to verify: correct COM port in Device Manager; set terminal to 9600 8N1 (8 data bits; No parity; 1 stop bit; No flow control); test with a known-good adapter if garbling persists
choice_c: Garbled output always means the console cable is wired as a straight-through instead of rollover
choice_d: Garbled characters mean the router's IOS has crashed and the console output is a memory dump
explanation: Console troubleshooting checklist: (1) Device Manager → Ports (COM & LPT) → verify adapter appears and note COM number. (2) Terminal (PuTTY/SecureCRT): Speed=9600; Data bits=8; Parity=None; Stop bits=1; Flow control=None. (3) If still garbled: try 115200 baud (some devices default higher). (4) Try a different USB port (driver re-enumeration). (5) Confirm rollover cable — pin 1-8 of one end connects to pin 8-1 on other end. Garbled output = baud mismatch most often; no output at all = wrong COM port or cable.
aspect_key: start/stop/parity/data bits; baud rate; signal levels

question: A Wireshark capture on a switch port shows thousands of DHCP Discover packets flooding the network every few seconds. No new devices are connecting. What is the most likely network condition causing this and what is the underlying DHCP/protocol behavior explaining it?
choice_a: The switch port has a duplex mismatch causing DHCP Discovers to be retransmitted
choice_b: A DHCP pool exhaustion condition — the DHCP scope has run out of available addresses; every Discover is responded to with a DHCP NAK (or no response) causing clients to immediately retransmit; this creates a Discover flood; simultaneously clients on expired leases fall to APIPA then immediately restart the DORA process creating a self-sustaining flood; the flood amplifies the problem by consuming network bandwidth
choice_c: DHCP Discover flooding is normal behavior and indicates healthy network activity
choice_d: The flooding is caused by a misconfigured DNS server returning incorrect addresses
explanation: DHCP exhaustion diagnosis: (1) Open DHCP server console → check scope statistics — if Active Leases = Total Addresses the pool is exhausted. (2) Check for rogue devices consuming addresses. (3) Check for short lease times causing rapid churn. (4) Check for DHCP snooping misconfiguration blocking Offers. Remediation: expand scope range; reduce lease time if high-churn environment; enable DHCP snooping; investigate unexpected device proliferation.
aspect_key: discover; offer; request; acknowledge

question: A network engineer must subnet 192.168.10.0/24 into subnets where each must support at least 30 hosts. Working through binary: what is the correct subnet mask; how many subnets result; and what are the first two subnet ranges?
choice_a: A /27 mask (255.255.255.224) provides 30 usable hosts — but this requires 6 host bits (2^6 − 2 = 62 usable) so the mask is actually /26
choice_b: Supporting 30 hosts requires at least 5 host bits (2^5 − 2 = 30 exactly; just sufficient) leaving 3 bits for subnets; mask = /27 (255.255.255.224); 8 subnets total (2^3); first two subnets: 192.168.10.0/27 (hosts .1-.30; broadcast .31) and 192.168.10.32/27 (hosts .33-.62; broadcast .63)
choice_c: A /28 mask supports 30 hosts because 28 + 4 = 32 total bits and 4 host bits = 16 addresses
choice_d: Supporting 30 hosts requires a /24 mask because smaller masks cannot guarantee 30 hosts per subnet
explanation: Binary subnet analysis: need 30 hosts → host bits needed = ceil(log2(32)) = 5 bits (2^5 = 32; minus 2 = 30 usable — exactly meets requirement). Network bits in last octet = 8−5 = 3 bits → /27 mask. Subnet increment = 2^5 = 32. Subnets: .0 (broadcast .31); .32 (broadcast .63); .64 (broadcast .95); .96 (.127); .128 (.159); .160 (.191); .192 (.223); .224 (.255). 8 subnets × 30 usable hosts = 240 usable addresses from the /24.
aspect_key: bit values define network/host boundaries in subnetting

question: An enterprise has a single DHCP server in the data center serving 20 VLANs on different subnets. Clients on VLANs 1 and 3 get addresses but clients on VLAN 2 cannot get any DHCP address despite the scope being active. What is the most likely missing configuration and why?
choice_a: The DHCP server has a firewall rule blocking requests from VLAN 2 specifically
choice_b: The Layer 3 switch or router interface for VLAN 2 is missing an IP helper address (DHCP relay agent configuration) — without it; DHCP Discover broadcasts from VLAN 2 clients are blocked at the router because routers do not forward broadcast traffic by default; the helper address unicasts the DHCP request to the centralized server on behalf of the VLAN 2 clients; VLANs 1 and 3 work because their interfaces already have helper addresses configured
choice_c: VLAN 2's scope has the wrong default gateway option; causing the server to reject VLAN 2 Discovers
choice_d: The DHCP server cannot serve more than 2 active scopes without a license upgrade
explanation: IP helper troubleshooting: verify Layer 3 VLAN interface configuration — interface Vlan2 → ip helper-address [DHCP server IP]. Check: show running-config interface vlan 2 → look for "ip helper-address." If missing: add the helper and test. Verification: client on VLAN 2 runs ipconfig /release; ipconfig /renew → should now receive address from the VLAN 2 scope. The server selects the correct scope based on the relay agent IP included in the forwarded packet.
aspect_key: per-subnet allocation boundaries and options

question: A host is configured with IP 10.1.1.200 and subnet mask 255.255.255.128. Using binary analysis: which subnet does this address belong to; what is the broadcast address; and can this host communicate directly with 10.1.1.130 without a router?
choice_a: 10.1.1.200 belongs to the 10.1.1.0/25 subnet and can communicate with 10.1.1.130 directly
choice_b: The mask 255.255.255.128 = /25 — last octet in binary: 10000000. Last octet of 10.1.1.200 = 11001000. The most significant bit (bit 7) = 1 → this host is in the 10.1.1.128/25 subnet (range .128 to .255; broadcast .255). 10.1.1.130 last octet = 10000010 — bit 7 = 1 → also in 10.1.1.128/25. Both hosts are in the same subnet so they can communicate directly via ARP without routing
choice_c: The mask 255.255.255.128 creates /26 subnets and 10.1.1.200 is in the 10.1.1.192/26 subnet
choice_d: 10.1.1.200 is in the 10.1.1.0/24 network so it can reach any 10.1.1.x address directly
explanation: Binary analysis: mask last octet = 1000 0000 = /25. 200 in binary = 1100 1000. AND with 1000 0000 = 1000 0000 = 128. Subnet = 10.1.1.128/25. Broadcast = 10.1.1.255 (all host bits = 1). 130 in binary = 1000 0010 → AND 1000 0000 = 1000 0000 = 128. Both in 10.1.1.128/25 → same subnet → direct ARP communication; no router needed.
aspect_key: bit values define network/host boundaries in subnetting

question: A security analyst uses Wireshark to capture traffic on an internal network switch and notices HTTP POST packets containing what appear to be username/password fields in plaintext. The web application uses HTTP on port 80. What does this reveal about the application's security posture; what attack is this vulnerable to; and what is the correct remediation?
choice_a: This is normal and expected — all web applications transmit form data in plaintext at layer 4
choice_b: HTTP transmits all data including form POST bodies in the clear TCP payload — any attacker with access to the network path (same switch; upstream router; or ISP) can capture and read credentials using Wireshark's Follow TCP Stream; this is a credential interception (on-path/MITM) vulnerability; the remediation is to enforce HTTPS (TLS) for all pages that accept or transmit credentials — TLS encrypts the application payload before TCP encapsulation rendering the captured bytes unreadable without session keys
choice_c: The vulnerability only exists on hub-connected networks; switches prevent this type of capture
choice_d: The fix is to change the HTTP port from 80 to 8080 which prevents capture by standard tools
explanation: Attack scenario: attacker on same VLAN captures traffic; follows TCP stream for port 80 to login endpoint; reads POST body "username=admin&password=secret123" in plaintext. Remediation: (1) Deploy TLS certificate on web server. (2) Configure HTTP→HTTPS redirect. (3) Set HSTS header (Strict-Transport-Security). (4) Verify HTTPS in Wireshark: TLSv1.3 Application Data — encrypted payload; credentials invisible. Network switches do not prevent this — SPAN ports; ARP poisoning; or rogue switch ports all enable capture.
aspect_key: installation; capture; and protocol visibility for packet analysis

question: A legacy Windows application requires WINS name resolution for NetBIOS hostnames and cannot be updated to use DNS. The enterprise has already decommissioned WINS servers. What are two technically valid options to provide NetBIOS name resolution without reinstalling WINS servers and what are the trade-offs of each?
choice_a: Legacy applications requiring WINS cannot function without a physical WINS server — no alternatives exist
choice_b: Option 1: configure LMHOSTS files on each client — a static text file mapping NetBIOS names to IPs; advantage: no server required; disadvantage: must be manually updated on every client when addresses change; does not scale. Option 2: configure DNS with legacy-compatible records — Windows DNS can handle NetBIOS-style flat name queries if search domains are configured to append the domain suffix; advantage: centrally managed; disadvantage: requires DNS changes and may not support all NetBIOS API calls that WINS handles natively
choice_c: The only solution is to re-enable WINS temporarily then migrate the application
choice_d: NetBIOS name resolution can be fully replaced by modifying the application's hosts file on the server side only
explanation: WINS alternatives: LMHOSTS (C:\Windows\System32\drivers\etc\lmhosts) — precursor to WINS; maps "FILESERVER  10.0.0.5  #PRE" entries. Loaded into cache at boot or on demand. Doesn't scale past ~20 hosts. DNS + search suffix: "FILESERVER" resolves as "FILESERVER.corp.example.com" via DNS — works for NET USE and UNC paths but may fail for NetBIOS APIs using broadcast. Third option: enable NetBIOS over TCP/IP and use broadcasts — only works within one subnet; no routing.
aspect_key: legacy microsoft name service preceding modern dns usage

question: A network shows high utilization on a single WAN link carrying both voice and data traffic. VoIP calls drop frequently during business hours. The link is 10 Mbps full-duplex. Wireshark shows RTP voice packets queued behind large TCP file transfer bursts of 1400-byte frames. What QoS mechanism addresses this and what is the specific problem it solves?
choice_a: Upgrade the WAN link to 100 Mbps — QoS cannot solve bandwidth saturation
choice_b: Low-Latency Queuing (LLQ) or priority queuing places RTP packets in a strict-priority queue that is serviced before data queues — the specific problem is serialization delay and queuing delay: at 10 Mbps a single 1400-byte Ethernet frame takes 1.12ms to serialize; when dozens of large frames are queued before voice packets each 40-byte RTP packet waits behind the entire burst adding tens of milliseconds of queuing delay that compounds jitter; LLQ drains the voice queue between every large frame burst
choice_c: Jitter buffers on the VoIP phones are sufficient to absorb any amount of queuing delay
choice_d: The solution is to change the TCP window size so large file transfers back off more quickly
explanation: Serialization delay math: 1400 bytes × 8 bits / 10 Mbps = 1.12ms per frame. 20 queued frames = 22.4ms of added delay for each voice packet behind them. At 64 kbps VoIP (G.711) packets are 20ms apart — 22ms of added jitter = voice packet arrives late. LLQ solution: voice gets strict priority; scheduler drains voice queue first; large data transfers use remaining bandwidth between voice bursts.
aspect_key: packet delay variation affecting real-time traffic quality

question: An administrator creates a DHCP scope of 192.168.5.1 to 192.168.5.254 on a Windows Server. The server's interface on the 192.168.5.0/24 network is 192.168.5.1 (static). Clients are receiving addresses but cannot reach the default gateway. The gateway is a router at 192.168.5.254. What is wrong and what must be fixed?
choice_a: The scope range must exclude the server's own IP address which is conflicting with client assignments
choice_b: The scope must have Option 3 (Router/Default Gateway) set to 192.168.5.254 — without this option clients receive an IP and mask but no gateway address; their routing table has no default route so they cannot reach any destination outside the local subnet; additionally the server's own IP (192.168.5.1) and the router's IP (192.168.5.254) should be excluded from the assignable range to prevent assignment conflicts
choice_c: The clients need DNS server addresses before they can reach the default gateway
choice_d: The default gateway cannot be on the same subnet as DHCP clients — it must be on a separate management VLAN
explanation: DHCP scope checklist: (1) Verify Option 3 is configured: scope → scope options → 003 Router → 192.168.5.254. (2) Add exclusions: 192.168.5.1 (server itself) and 192.168.5.254 (router) so DHCP doesn't assign these to clients. (3) After fix: client runs ipconfig /renew → receives gateway. (4) Verify: ipconfig /all shows "Default Gateway: 192.168.5.254" and route print shows 0.0.0.0 via 192.168.5.254.
aspect_key: per-subnet allocation boundaries and options

question: A Wireshark capture at a corporate exit router shows that an internal client is making unencrypted DNS queries for external hostnames to 8.8.8.8 (Google DNS) instead of the internal DNS server at 10.0.0.5. The internal DNS server is supposed to forward external queries to 8.8.8.8. What is the problem; what security risk does this create; and how is it remediated?
choice_a: Clients querying 8.8.8.8 directly is normal and preferred because Google DNS is faster
choice_b: The client is bypassing the internal DNS server — its DNS configuration points directly to 8.8.8.8 rather than 10.0.0.5; this creates a security risk: (1) internal DNS filtering/blocking policies are bypassed — clients can reach domains the enterprise has blocked; (2) DNS query logs on the internal server are incomplete — security events go undetected; remediation: force DNS to internal server via DHCP Option 6; add a firewall rule blocking outbound UDP/TCP port 53 to any destination except the internal DNS server
choice_c: DNS queries to external servers are encrypted so there is no security risk
choice_d: The internal DNS server should be deleted and all clients configured to use 8.8.8.8 directly for better performance
explanation: DNS enforcement architecture: DHCP Option 6 → pushes 10.0.0.5 as DNS server to all clients. Firewall rule: DENY outbound UDP/TCP 53 from internal hosts except 10.0.0.5 → 8.8.8.8 (forcing all DNS through the internal forwarder). Internal server logs all queries for security monitoring. DNS filtering blocks malware C2 domains. Clients with manually set 8.8.8.8 are caught by the firewall rule.
aspect_key: upstream resolver for unresolved/non-local queries

question: Two network devices are connected with one end forced to 100 Mbps full-duplex and the other end set to autonegotiate. The autonegotiating end detects 100 Mbps but defaults to half-duplex per IEEE 802.3 parallel detection. Throughput between the two devices is measured at 12 Mbps on a 100 Mbps link. Explain the exact mechanism causing this and how the CRC error counters would appear on each side.
choice_a: Both devices see identical CRC error rates because both sides experience the same collisions
choice_b: The full-duplex end transmits while the half-duplex end is also transmitting — the full-duplex device does not detect this as a collision (it has no CSMA/CD because it is in full-duplex mode); the half-duplex end detects a collision and executes CSMA/CD backoff; late collisions occur at the half-duplex side because its transmissions collide with the full-duplex end's transmissions mid-frame; the half-duplex side shows high CRC and late collision errors while the full-duplex side shows runts and FCS errors from frames corrupted by the collision; throughput collapses to 10-20% of nominal capacity
choice_c: Only the full-duplex side shows errors because half-duplex devices are collision-tolerant
choice_d: The half-duplex end automatically switches to full-duplex after 30 seconds to resolve the mismatch
explanation: Duplex mismatch error profile: half-duplex side: late collisions (frame collision detected after 64 bytes transmitted — only possible in duplex mismatch); input errors; CRC errors; collisions counter incrementing. Full-duplex side: runts (frames truncated by collision); input CRC errors; no collision counter (FD has none). Throughput: 100 Mbps drops to 10-20% due to constant backoff. Diagnosis: check interface counters — "late collisions" is the signature symptom.
aspect_key: simplex; half-duplex; full-duplex communication behavior

question: A 192.168.20.0/24 network must be divided into exactly 6 subnets with the maximum number of hosts possible per subnet. Using binary analysis: what mask should be used; how many usable hosts per subnet does it provide; and what are the first three subnet addresses?
choice_a: A /26 mask creates 4 subnets — not enough; a /27 creates 8 subnets; the first three are .0; .32; .64
choice_b: To create at least 6 subnets requires 3 subnet bits (2^3 = 8 subnets; 2^2 = 4 is insufficient); 3 subnet bits in the last octet leaves 5 host bits; the mask is /27 (255.255.255.224); usable hosts per subnet = 2^5 − 2 = 30; first three subnet addresses: 192.168.20.0/27; 192.168.20.32/27; 192.168.20.64/27
choice_c: A /25 mask creates 6 subnets because 25+6 = 31 bits total
choice_d: 3 subnet bits creates exactly 6 subnets because each bit doubles the count — 3 bits = 6
explanation: Binary subnet design: need ≥6 subnets → bits needed = 3 (2^3 = 8 ≥ 6; 2^2 = 4 < 6). Subnet bits in last octet: 3 → mask last octet = 11100000 = 224 → 255.255.255.224 = /27. Host bits = 5 → 32 addresses per subnet; 30 usable. Increment = 32. Subnets: .0 (range .1-.30; bcast .31); .32 (range .33-.62; bcast .63); .64 (range .65-.94; bcast .95); .96; .128; .160; .192; .224.
aspect_key: bit values define network/host boundaries in subnetting

question: An enterprise DNS server is configured as the authoritative server for corp.example.com. The zone's SOA record has a serial number of 2024010101 and a refresh interval of 3600 seconds. A secondary DNS server has serial 2024010101. An administrator updates multiple A records but forgets to increment the SOA serial number. What happens and why?
choice_a: The secondary server detects record changes directly and replicates them within 5 minutes regardless of serial number
choice_b: The secondary server queries the primary at the next refresh interval (3600 seconds = 1 hour); compares the SOA serial number; sees no change (2024010101 = 2024010101) and concludes the zone has not been updated; it does not perform a zone transfer; the new A records remain on the primary only and the secondary continues serving stale data; clients directed to the secondary get old IP addresses for updated hostnames; fix: increment SOA serial (e.g. to 2024010102) and trigger an immediate NOTIFY to the secondary
choice_c: The secondary always performs a full zone transfer at each refresh interval regardless of serial number
choice_d: Secondary DNS servers poll the changed records individually rather than comparing serial numbers
explanation: SOA serial mechanics: secondary compares its serial to primary's SOA serial. If primary serial > secondary serial → full zone transfer (AXFR) or incremental (IXFR). If equal → no transfer needed. Serial must ALWAYS be incremented on zone changes — convention is YYYYMMDDNN (date + sequence). Forgetting the increment is a classic DNS admin mistake causing split-brain. NOTIFY (RFC 1996) allows the primary to proactively alert secondaries of changes without waiting for refresh.
aspect_key: recursive resolution; authoritative responses; and caching

question: A DHCP server has a scope of 10.10.10.50 to 10.10.10.200 but a network printer at 10.10.10.150 (statically configured) intermittently loses its IP configuration. Investigation shows the printer receives a DHCP Offer for 10.10.10.150. Explain the complete chain of events causing this and the two correct solutions.
choice_a: The printer's static IP was assigned by DHCP and cannot conflict with the scope range
choice_b: The DHCP server is assigning 10.10.10.150 to a different client because the address falls within the scope range — when the DHCP server performs a ping check before offering an IP it should detect the statically configured printer; but if ping checks are disabled or the printer fails to respond to ICMP (firewall blocks ping); the server treats the address as free and offers it to another client; when the second client configures 10.10.10.150 both devices share the same IP causing periodic ARP conflicts; correct solutions: (1) add 10.10.10.150 as a scope exclusion; or (2) create a DHCP reservation for the printer's MAC address so the server always assigns .150 to the printer specifically
choice_c: The printer's static IP is in a different subnet than the DHCP scope so no conflict can occur
choice_d: DHCP servers never assign IP addresses that are already in use — this situation is technically impossible
explanation: IP conflict scenario: scope .50-.200 includes .150. Ping check disabled → server offers .150 to client. Client A configures .150. Printer already has .150. ARP table conflict → whichever device ARPs last wins; the loser loses connectivity intermittently. Exclusion fix: scope exclusion → DHCP never offers .150. Reservation fix: MAC 00:11:22:33:44:55 always gets .150 from DHCP (remove static from printer; use reservation instead). Best practice: exclude the entire static IP range from DHCP pool.
aspect_key: fixed ip lease tied to device mac

question: During a Wireshark capture of a Telnet session to a router a network student uses Follow TCP Stream and sees all router commands and outputs including the enable password typed during login. The router uses password encryption with service password-encryption. Explain why the password is still visible in Wireshark despite the router's password encryption.
choice_a: Service password-encryption uses AES-256 which cannot be decrypted by Wireshark
choice_b: Service password-encryption on Cisco IOS encrypts the password in the router's configuration file (running-config/startup-config) using a weak reversible Vigenere cipher (type 7) — it prevents shoulder surfing of the config but has no effect on the Telnet session itself; Telnet transmits all data including authentication exchanges in plaintext over TCP; Wireshark captures the TCP payload directly and the password appears as cleartext characters in the stream because Telnet never encrypts its network traffic regardless of how the password is stored on the device
choice_c: Wireshark can only see the enable password because it uses type 5 encryption; login passwords are invisible
choice_d: Service password-encryption encrypts both the stored password and the session traffic making them equally secure
explanation: Layered confusion: router stores "enable password cisco" as "enable password 7 0822455D0A16" in config (type 7 = weak cipher; easily crackable). But the TELNET PROTOCOL transmits the password as ASCII over the TCP socket — storage encryption is irrelevant to transmission. Wireshark Follow TCP Stream shows every keystroke. This is why Cisco recommends enable secret (MD5/SHA-256 hash) for storage AND SSH (TLS-encrypted transport) to replace Telnet — both layers must be secured independently.
aspect_key: installation; capture; and protocol visibility for packet analysis

question: A DNS recursive resolver is queried for the FQDN www.example.com. The resolver has no cached entry. Walk through the exact sequence of queries the resolver makes; what information each server returns; and what the resolver does with the final answer before sending it to the client.
choice_a: The resolver sends one query directly to the authoritative server for example.com
choice_b: Step 1: resolver queries a root name server "Who is authoritative for .com?" — root returns NS records pointing to the .com TLD servers (e.g. a.gtld-servers.net). Step 2: resolver queries the .com TLD server "Who is authoritative for example.com?" — TLD returns NS records for example.com's authoritative servers (e.g. ns1.example.com). Step 3: resolver queries ns1.example.com "What is the A record for www.example.com?" — authoritative server returns the IP address (e.g. 93.184.216.34) with a TTL. Step 4: resolver caches the A record for the TTL duration and returns the IP to the original client; the client connects to 93.184.216.34
choice_c: The resolver queries the TLD server first because .com is in the FQDN
choice_d: The resolver contacts the client's ISP which stores all DNS records globally
explanation: Full DNS resolution walk: priming query (if needed) → root hints give root server IPs. Resolver → root: "www.example.com?" → root: "I don't know; ask .com TLD at [IPs]." Resolver → .com TLD: "www.example.com?" → TLD: "I don't know; ask ns1.example.com at [IP]." Resolver → ns1.example.com: "www.example.com?" → authoritative: "93.184.216.34; TTL 3600." Resolver caches; returns to client. Client connects to 93.184.216.34.
aspect_key: root; tld; domain; host/subdomain levels

question: A T1 circuit uses TDM with 24 channels and operates at 1.544 Mbps total. Each channel carries digitized voice at 64 Kbps. A network engineer notices that 18 of the 24 channels are carrying voice but the remaining 6 channels are unused. What is the total wasted bandwidth in Mbps and what does this reveal about TDM's limitation compared to statistical multiplexing?
choice_a: No bandwidth is wasted in TDM because unused channels are automatically reassigned
choice_b: Each unused TDM channel permanently occupies its allocated 64 Kbps time slot — 6 unused channels × 64 Kbps = 384 Kbps of wasted bandwidth even though no traffic is being carried; in statistical multiplexing the same idle capacity would be available to active streams; TDM's fixed slot assignment is efficient for constant-bit-rate traffic like voice but wasteful for bursty data where utilization is uneven — this is why packet-switched networks replaced circuit-switched TDM for data traffic
choice_c: TDM cannot carry voice traffic — 64 Kbps channels are only used for data
choice_d: Unused TDM channels consume zero bandwidth because they contain only timing signals
explanation: T1 TDM math: 24 channels × 64 Kbps + 8 Kbps framing = 1.544 Mbps total. 6 idle channels × 64 Kbps = 384 Kbps wasted = 24.8% waste. Statistical mux alternative: those 6 idle channels' bandwidth is available to the 18 active channels — each active channel could burst to 85.8 Kbps (1.536 Mbps / 18) instead of being capped at 64 Kbps. This is the efficiency argument for VoIP over packet networks.
aspect_key: split by frequency bands vs split by time slots

question: A client machine receives a DHCP lease and immediately after receiving the ACK sends a gratuitous ARP. The gratuitous ARP receives a reply from another device on the segment. What does this indicate; what should the client do; and what RFC behavior specifies this?
choice_a: The gratuitous ARP reply means the DHCP server made an error; the client should reject the lease and manually configure an IP
choice_b: A gratuitous ARP reply means another device already has the IP address the DHCP server just assigned — an IP address conflict exists; the client should send a DHCPDECLINE message to the DHCP server informing it the address is in conflict; the server marks the address as bad and should not re-offer it; the client then restarts the DORA process to obtain a different address; RFC 2131 specifies this DHCPDECLINE behavior for conflict detection
choice_c: A gratuitous ARP reply is expected and means the network switch confirmed the address
choice_d: The client should keep the address since DHCP's offer takes legal precedence over any existing static configuration
explanation: DHCPDECLINE flow: client receives ACK → sends gratuitous ARP (source IP = offered IP; target IP = offered IP) → if a reply arrives: conflict detected → client sends DHCPDECLINE to server (with the conflicting IP identified) → server marks IP as BAD ADDRESS (excluded from future offers) → client waits and restarts DORA. RFC 2131: "the client SHOULD probe the newly received address." Windows uses an ARP probe; Linux uses arping. The server should also log the conflict for admin review.
aspect_key: discover; offer; request; acknowledge

question: An administrator is designing DHCP for a network with 300 workstations on 192.168.0.0/24. The scope must reserve 192.168.0.1-20 for infrastructure (routers; switches; servers) and 192.168.0.230-254 for printers with static IPs. What scope range and exclusions should be configured and how many workstations can the DHCP pool support?
choice_a: Configure the scope as 192.168.0.1-254 with no exclusions — DHCP conflict detection handles the rest
choice_b: Configure the scope as 192.168.0.1 to 192.168.0.254; add exclusions for 192.168.0.1-20 (infrastructure) and 192.168.0.230-254 (printers) — this leaves 192.168.0.21 to 192.168.0.229 as the dynamic pool which provides 209 assignable addresses; since there are 300 workstations and only 209 addresses are available the design fails — the network requires a larger address space (e.g. a /23 supernet providing 510 usable addresses) or the subnet must be redesigned
choice_c: Configure the scope as 192.168.0.21-229 with no exclusions — exclusions are only needed for WINS servers
choice_d: Configure the scope as the full 192.168.0.0-255 range and let DHCP auto-detect conflicts via ping checks
explanation: Scope capacity analysis: /24 = 254 usable addresses (excluding .0 and .255). Infrastructure exclusion: 20 addresses (.1-.20). Printer exclusion: 25 addresses (.230-.254). Dynamic pool: .21-.229 = 209 addresses. Need: 300 workstations. Shortfall: 91 addresses. Resolution options: (1) expand to /23 (192.168.0.0-192.168.1.255 = 510 usable); (2) implement DHCP with short lease times so addresses are reused (if not all 300 online simultaneously); (3) reassign the printer range to reduce exclusions.
aspect_key: per-subnet allocation boundaries and options

question: A network administrator is troubleshooting why a newly deployed application server cannot be reached by its FQDN server01.prod.corp.example.com but CAN be reached by IP address directly. The DNS A record was just created. What are the three most likely causes to check in order of likelihood?
choice_a: The firewall is blocking DNS queries to the server — check the server's firewall rules
choice_b: The three most likely causes in order: (1) DNS propagation delay — the A record was just created; client resolvers may have a cached NXDOMAIN (negative cache) for the name with its own TTL preventing immediate resolution; (2) the A record was created in the wrong DNS zone — verify the record is in prod.corp.example.com zone not example.com; (3) the client's DNS server is not the authoritative server for this zone and has no forwarder configured to reach it — verify the client's configured DNS server can actually resolve names in corp.example.com
choice_c: The server's network interface is not configured to respond to FQDNs — only IP addresses
choice_d: DNS can only be tested from Windows clients; Linux and Mac clients cannot resolve enterprise FQDNs
explanation: DNS troubleshooting sequence: (1) nslookup server01.prod.corp.example.com [DNS-server-IP] — tests resolution from specific server. (2) If NXDOMAIN: check authoritative zone for the record — DNS console or dig @ns1.corp.example.com server01.prod.corp.example.com. (3) Negative cache: check negative TTL in SOA. (4) Wrong zone: common mistake — creating A record in parent zone (example.com) instead of delegated zone (corp.example.com). (5) Verify delegation: dig NS corp.example.com — confirms zone delegation is correct.
aspect_key: recursive resolution; authoritative responses; and caching

question: A host at 172.16.50.100/255.255.240.0 attempts to ping 172.16.60.200. Determine through binary analysis whether these addresses are on the same subnet or different subnets and explain the routing decision.
choice_a: Both addresses are in different /24 subnets because the third octets (50 and 60) are different
choice_b: The mask 255.255.240.0 = /20 — the third octet mask bits are 11110000; performing a bitwise AND: 50 AND 11110000 = 00110010 AND 11110000 = 00110000 = 48; network address for 172.16.50.100 = 172.16.48.0/20; for 172.16.60.200: 60 AND 11110000 = 00111100 AND 11110000 = 00110000 = 48; network address = 172.16.48.0/20; both addresses produce the same network address so they are on the SAME subnet — the host sends an ARP request for 172.16.60.200 rather than sending to the gateway
choice_c: The addresses are in different subnets because 50 ≠ 60 in the third octet
choice_d: A /20 mask only allows subnetting of the first two octets; the third octet is always in the same subnet
explanation: /20 binary analysis: mask = 255.255.240.0 = 11111111.11111111.11110000.00000000. Third octet 50 = 00110010 AND 11110000 = 00110000 = 48. Third octet 60 = 00111100 AND 11110000 = 00110000 = 48. Both yield 172.16.48.0/20 (range 172.16.48.0 to 172.16.63.255). Both are in the same /20 subnet — ping goes directly via ARP. This is why you cannot determine subnet membership by just looking at octet values without applying the mask.
aspect_key: bit values define network/host boundaries in subnetting

question: A cable TV system uses FDM to deliver 500 channels over a single coaxial cable to homes. Each TV channel occupies a 6 MHz frequency band. What is the total spectrum required for 500 channels; why can the same physical cable deliver all channels simultaneously; and what happens at the subscriber's television?
choice_a: The cable must use TDM rather than FDM because 500 channels cannot fit in the available spectrum simultaneously
choice_b: 500 channels × 6 MHz per channel = 3000 MHz (3 GHz) of total spectrum; FDM allows all 500 channels to be transmitted simultaneously on different frequency bands within this 3 GHz range without interference; at the television the tuner circuit acts as a bandpass filter — it selects only the 6 MHz band corresponding to the desired channel and rejects all other frequencies; the subscriber only sees one channel at a time but all channels are physically present on the cable simultaneously
choice_c: FDM cable TV transmits channels in sequence; the TV buffers them to create the illusion of simultaneity
choice_d: Each channel requires a separate coaxial cable — combining them on one cable would cause interference
explanation: FDM cable TV: coax supports ~5 MHz to 1 GHz typically (older systems); newer DOCSIS 3.1 uses up to 1.2 GHz. Channels stacked in 6 MHz slots: channel 2 at 54-60 MHz; channel 3 at 60-66 MHz; etc. TV tuner: voltage-controlled oscillator selects desired center frequency; bandpass filter extracts that 6 MHz slice; demodulator extracts video signal. Data (internet) coexists on the same cable using separate frequency bands above the TV channels.
aspect_key: split by frequency bands vs split by time slots

question: A network engineer is asked to explain why using 9600 baud rather than 115200 baud on a console connection does not significantly affect the administrative experience. Calculate the character throughput at each rate and identify the practical context that makes this difference irrelevant.
choice_a: Higher baud rates are always better — 115200 is required for any Cisco device configuration
choice_b: At 9600 baud with 8N1 (10 bits per character including start/stop): 9600 / 10 = 960 characters/second. At 115200 baud: 115200 / 10 = 11520 characters/second. Console sessions involve an administrator reading and typing — a skilled typist types ~100 characters/minute = 1.67 characters/second; the screen output of "show running-config" is ~5000 characters which takes 0.43 seconds at 115200 vs 5.2 seconds at 9600; for interactive command entry the difference is imperceptible since the bottleneck is human reading speed not the serial link
choice_c: 9600 baud cannot carry configuration commands — it is only for viewing log messages
choice_d: The baud rate affects the router's CPU load; 115200 significantly increases router overhead
explanation: Practical impact: 9600 baud is sufficient for interactive CLI use because human interaction rate << serial throughput. The noticeable difference: large "show" command outputs scroll slightly faster at 115200. Boot messages display faster at 115200 (bootloader output can be 100KB = 104 seconds at 9600 vs 8.7 seconds at 115200). Common practice: Cisco defaults to 9600 for maximum compatibility with older terminal equipment; many technicians upgrade to 115200 for faster boot visibility.
aspect_key: start/stop/parity/data bits; baud rate; signal levels

question: DHCP snooping is enabled on a switch and configured to trust only the uplink port connected to the distribution layer. A help desk ticket reports that workstations on access ports are suddenly not getting DHCP addresses. A Wireshark capture on an access port shows DHCP Offer and Acknowledge packets arriving on the port but workstations ignore them. What is the actual behavior of DHCP snooping causing this and what is the legitimate scenario where this would protect the network?
choice_a: DHCP snooping drops all DHCP packets on access ports including both client packets and server responses
choice_b: DHCP snooping drops DHCP Offer and Acknowledge packets arriving on untrusted (access) ports — the switch hardware drops them before they reach clients; this is the intended protective behavior: if an unauthorized DHCP server is plugged into an access port it cannot deliver Offers to clients; the Wireshark capture shows the packets arriving at the port but the switch discards them before forwarding; the legitimate scenario is a rogue DHCP server connected to an access port that would otherwise provide incorrect gateway and DNS settings to hijack client traffic
choice_c: DHCP snooping only inspects UDP port 67 packets; Offer and Acknowledge use port 68 and are not affected
choice_d: Workstations ignore DHCP Offers from untrusted ports by design in the client's TCP/IP stack
explanation: DHCP snooping mechanics: untrusted port receives DHCP Offer/ACK → switch drops them (rogue server protection). Trusted port (uplink): all DHCP messages pass. Issue in scenario: if Offers are visible in Wireshark but clients get nothing → snooping is dropping them in the forwarding hardware before delivery. Diagnosis: check "show ip dhcp snooping binding" and "show ip dhcp snooping statistics" — drop counter will be incrementing. Fix: ensure the uplink toward the DHCP server is configured as trusted.
aspect_key: discover; offer; request; acknowledge

question: An FQDN in a zone file is written as mail.corp.example.com without a trailing dot and the zone's $ORIGIN directive is set to example.com. What IP address does the A record for mail.corp.example.com actually point to and what is the common misconfiguration error here?
choice_a: The trailing dot has no effect in zone files — mail.corp.example.com and mail.corp.example.com. are identical
choice_b: Without a trailing dot in a BIND zone file a hostname is treated as relative to the $ORIGIN — mail.corp.example.com without a trailing dot becomes mail.corp.example.com.example.com (the $ORIGIN appended); this is a common misconfiguration that creates a nonsensical FQDN that does not resolve; the administrator intended to create a record for mail.corp.example.com but actually created one for mail.corp.example.com.example.com; the fix is to append a trailing dot: mail.corp.example.com. (absolute) or use the relative label "mail.corp" which BIND appends $ORIGIN to create the intended FQDN
choice_c: BIND zone files ignore the $ORIGIN directive for subdomains — it only applies to the top-level domain name
choice_d: The trailing dot only matters for MX records; A records are always treated as absolute
explanation: Zone file relative vs absolute: $ORIGIN example.com. (note trailing dot on origin itself). "mail.corp.example.com" without dot → BIND appends origin → "mail.corp.example.com.example.com." — wrong. "mail.corp.example.com." with trailing dot → absolute → correct. "mail" (short label) → BIND appends origin → "mail.example.com." — also wrong for a subdomain. Correct: "mail.corp" → "mail.corp.example.com." OR use full absolute FQDN with trailing dot. This is a source of many DNS misconfiguration bugs.
aspect_key: host label + domain hierarchy identifies exact target

question: A network has 200 devices that need IP addresses. The administrator assigned them static IPs documented in a spreadsheet. Six months later an audit reveals 15 IP conflicts and 22 undocumented devices on the network. Explain the specific failure mode that led to this state and contrast it with how DHCP with reservations would have prevented both problems.
choice_a: Static IP is inherently more reliable than DHCP because each device has a permanently recorded address
choice_b: Static IP depends on human discipline for accuracy — the specific failure modes are: (1) a new device was added and the technician chose an IP already in use (the spreadsheet was not checked or was outdated) causing conflicts; (2) devices were added without being documented in the spreadsheet resulting in undocumented hosts; DHCP with reservations prevents both: the DHCP server is the single authoritative IP registry — any device receiving a lease appears in the DHCP lease database; reservation entries document each device's MAC-to-IP mapping; IP conflicts are impossible because the server controls assignment centrally
choice_c: DHCP reservations have the same documentation problems as static IPs because reservations must be manually entered
choice_d: DHCP cannot prevent IP conflicts because two DHCP servers can assign the same address
explanation: Static IP risk: distributed assignment = distributed error risk. One technician uses .50 because the spreadsheet shows it free; another uses .50 because they didn't check. DHCP centralization: server logs show every lease; every device; every MAC address. Audit is trivial — open DHCP console → see all assigned addresses. Reservation audit: compare reservation list to expected device inventory. Conflict prevention: the server assigns from its pool; the pool entry is marked used; another request cannot receive the same IP (unless rogue DHCP or overlapping scopes).
aspect_key: fixed ip lease tied to device mac

question: A network manager claims that adding a second DHCP server to the same subnet as a failover measure will automatically double the available address pool. Explain why this claim is technically incorrect and describe the correct way to implement DHCP redundancy.
choice_a: Adding a second DHCP server doubles the address pool because each server manages a separate /25 subnet
choice_b: Adding a second DHCP server with an overlapping scope does not double the pool — it creates a rogue server condition where both servers may offer the same address to different clients causing IP conflicts; the correct approach for Windows DHCP failover uses the DHCP failover protocol (RFC 3074 / Windows Server 2012+) where two servers share a single authoritative scope in either hot standby mode (one active; one passive) or load balancing mode (each server handles a percentage of requests from the same scope) — both servers are synchronized and the scope is not doubled but made redundant
choice_c: Two DHCP servers on the same subnet work cooperatively by default because DHCP uses multicast
choice_d: DHCP failover requires identical lease databases and is only available on Linux DHCP servers
explanation: Correct DHCP HA: Windows DHCP failover (Server 2012+): two servers partner; share lease database; synchronize state. Load balance mode: 50/50 split of client requests. Hot standby: partner activates only if primary fails. Both modes use the SAME scope — not two separate scopes. Alternative: split scope (80/20) — primary server has 80% of range; secondary has 20%; no protocol synchronization needed but less efficient. The key: controlled split; not uncoordinated dual scopes.
aspect_key: per-subnet allocation boundaries and options

question: A network student is learning to convert IP addresses to binary for subnetting. The address 192.168.100.205 must be ANDed with subnet mask 255.255.255.192 to find the network address. Show the complete binary conversion and AND operation for the last two octets and identify the network address and host number.
choice_a: The network address is 192.168.100.192 and the host number is 13 but this requires no binary calculation
choice_b: Last two octets: address 100 = 01100100; mask 255 = 11111111; AND = 01100100 = 100 → third octet network = 100. Address 205 = 11001101; mask 192 = 11000000; AND = 11000000 = 192 → fourth octet network = 192. Network address = 192.168.100.192/26. Host bits = 205 AND NOT(192) = 205 AND 63 = 11001101 AND 00111111 = 00001101 = 13. Host number = 13 (range 1-62 in this /26 subnet)
choice_c: Subnetting the last octet is impossible without converting the entire 32-bit address to binary
choice_d: The AND operation uses OR not AND — the network address is found by ORing the IP with the mask
explanation: Complete calculation: Address last octet: 205 = 128+64+8+4+1 = 11001101. Mask: 192 = 128+64 = 11000000. AND: 11001101 AND 11000000 = 11000000 = 192. Network = 192.168.100.192/26. Host bits (lower 6): 001101 = 8+4+1 = 13. Subnet range: .192 (network) to .255 (broadcast). Usable hosts: .193 to .254 = 62 hosts. This address is host #13 of subnet 192.168.100.192/26.
aspect_key: bit values define network/host boundaries in subnetting

question: A company's internal DNS server is authoritative for corp.company.com and uses a forwarder to 8.8.8.8 for external queries. The forwarder stops responding. Describe the complete sequence of events affecting DNS resolution for internal and external names and what the administrator should do.
choice_a: Only external DNS queries fail; internal queries are unaffected because they don't use the forwarder
choice_b: Internal queries (corp.company.com records): still resolved locally from the authoritative zone — unaffected. External queries (google.com; microsoft.com etc.): the server tries the forwarder 8.8.8.8; times out after the forwarder timeout period (typically 5 seconds per attempt × configured retry count); if no root hints are configured the query fails with SERVFAIL; applications waiting for external DNS hang for the timeout duration before failing; users experience slow browser loading then DNS failures for external sites. Administrator action: (1) add a backup forwarder (e.g. 1.1.1.1) to the forwarder list; (2) configure root hints as a fallback if forwarders all fail; (3) investigate 8.8.8.8 connectivity
choice_c: All DNS queries fail including internal names when the forwarder is down
choice_d: The internal DNS server automatically switches to using root hints when the forwarder fails without any administrator intervention
explanation: DNS forwarder failure impact: internal zones = authoritative → served directly; no forwarder needed. External zones = dependent on forwarder → failure = 5s+ timeout per query. Forwarder timeout configured in DNS server settings. Fallback to root hints: Windows DNS can be configured to use root hints if all forwarders fail (checkbox: "Use root hints if no forwarders are available"). Best practice: always configure 2+ forwarders for redundancy — if 8.8.8.8 fails; 1.1.1.1 takes over.
aspect_key: upstream resolver for unresolved/non-local queries

question: A duplex mismatch exists between a server NIC (forced full-duplex 1 Gbps) and a switch port (autonegotiating; falls to 1 Gbps half-duplex). The server is a database server handling 200 concurrent client connections. Explain in detail why this specific scenario is more damaging than the same mismatch on a workstation connection.
choice_a: A duplex mismatch on a database server is identical in impact to one on a workstation
choice_b: The database server's 200 concurrent connections generate continuous bidirectional traffic — every time a client submits a query and the server responds the half-duplex NIC (from the switch perspective) experiences collisions because the server is transmitting while the switch is also sending; each collision triggers exponential backoff pausing all 200 connections simultaneously; database queries that complete in 5ms at full-duplex may take 50-200ms due to backoff overhead; connection pools time out; applications report database errors; this compounds because each slow query holds a connection longer increasing queue depth and amplifying the overload; a workstation with one user is barely affected because human interaction intervals hide the 1-2ms backoff periods
choice_c: Database servers use dedicated backup links that bypass the switch so duplex mismatches don't affect them
choice_d: A duplex mismatch only affects network speeds not application response times
explanation: Compounding effect: database server = high-traffic sustained bidirectional sender. Collision frequency = proportional to tx/rx overlap probability. At 200 concurrent connections: near-continuous simultaneous tx+rx → nearly every transmission collides → near-constant backoff. Backoff effect on connection pool: pool timeout (e.g. 30 seconds) × collision frequency → connections dropped → application errors. Workstation: user types → waits → reads → collision probability = low. Fix: force both sides to 1G full-duplex; never let the server port autonegotiate.
aspect_key: simplex; half-duplex; full-duplex communication behavior

question: A DHCP lease is set to 7 days. A laptop connects on Monday and receives a lease. On Wednesday the laptop is sent for repair and taken off the network without releasing its lease. On the following Monday a new laptop needs the same IP (it is the last available address in the pool). What happens and why?
choice_a: The DHCP server reclaims the IP immediately when it detects the laptop is offline via ICMP ping
choice_b: The original lease does not expire until Sunday (7 days from Monday) — the DHCP server cannot reclaim it because it has no mechanism to detect the laptop went offline without releasing its lease; the server only reclaims addresses at T2 (87.5% = ~6.1 days = Sunday morning) when the client fails to rebind; until then the address is unavailable; the new laptop on Monday either cannot get an address (pool exhausted) or — if the lease expired Sunday — can receive the now-freed address; to prevent this in high-churn environments administrators use shorter lease times or enable DHCP server-side ping probes before reclaiming expired addresses
choice_c: DHCP servers actively ping all leased addresses every hour and reclaim those that do not respond
choice_d: The new laptop automatically negotiates with the old laptop to share the IP address
explanation: Lease lifecycle without release: normal release (ipconfig /release; graceful shutdown) → DHCP marks address free immediately. Abnormal disconnect (power failure; removal) → address stays leased. Server reclaim: only at lease expiry. 7-day lease: address tied up from Monday to following Monday regardless of device state. Prevention: (1) configure DHCP conflict detection (ping before expiry reassignment); (2) shorter leases for mobile-device-heavy environments; (3) users educated to run ipconfig /release before removal.
aspect_key: duration controls churn and address reuse behavior

question: A network captures DNS traffic and shows a client sending queries with the RD (Recursion Desired) bit set to 0. The client is receiving referral responses with NS records rather than final A record answers. What type of DNS query is being used; why would a client intentionally do this; and what is the normal client behavior?
choice_a: Clients setting RD=0 have a misconfigured TCP/IP stack — this should always be RD=1
choice_b: RD=0 means the client is sending iterative queries — the server returns its best answer (often a referral to another authoritative server) without doing the recursive resolution itself; a client would intentionally send iterative queries when it IS a DNS server performing recursive resolution on behalf of other clients — a recursive resolver queries root; TLD; and authoritative servers iteratively; it receives referrals at each step and follows them itself; normal end-user clients always set RD=1 (recursive) because they want a final answer not referrals; the captured traffic is from the DNS resolver component; not an end-user application
choice_c: RD=0 and RD=1 are identical — the DNS server always performs full recursion regardless of the flag
choice_d: Setting RD=0 is a security feature that prevents DNS amplification attacks
explanation: DNS flag RD (Recursion Desired) bit 8 of flags field: RD=1 → client says "please resolve this fully for me." RD=0 → client says "give me your best answer; I'll follow referrals myself." End-user OS resolver: always RD=1. DNS server recursive resolver: sends RD=0 to root/TLD/authoritative servers (they are authoritative; not recursive — they would reject RD=1 anyway because they are configured non-recursive). Wireshark: a DNS query with RD=0 from an IP that is itself a DNS server = expected resolver behavior.
aspect_key: recursive resolution; authoritative responses; and caching

question: An RS-232 console cable connects a laptop to a network switch. The laptop's terminal emulator shows output but every character appears doubled (e.g. typing "a" displays "aa"). What is the cause and how is it fixed?
choice_a: Doubled characters mean the cable is using a straight-through pinout instead of a rollover pinout
choice_b: The switch console port has local echo enabled — it is echoing every received character back to the terminal; simultaneously the terminal emulator's local echo setting is also displaying each typed character; both the terminal and the device are echoing resulting in doubled display; the fix is to disable local echo in the terminal emulator settings (the switch's echo is normal console behavior; disabling terminal echo removes the duplicate) OR disable terminal echo entirely and rely on the device's echo for display
choice_c: Doubled characters are caused by a baud rate mismatch — increase the baud rate to eliminate duplication
choice_d: Doubled characters indicate the RS-232 data bits setting is wrong — change from 8 to 7 data bits
explanation: Terminal echo settings: most terminal emulators (PuTTY; SecureCRT) have a "Local echo" setting. PuTTY: Connection → Terminal → Local echo → set to "Off (never)" when connecting to a device that echoes. If local echo = ON and device also echoes → double display. Diagnostic: type a character; if it appears twice = both echoing. Other echo scenarios: if nothing appears after typing = device is NOT echoing AND terminal echo is off. If the fix is SSH instead of Telnet: SSH uses pseudo-terminal negotiation to handle echo correctly.
aspect_key: start/stop/parity/data bits; baud rate; signal levels

question: A corporate network has users reporting that external websites load slowly but internal resources are fast. Ping to 8.8.8.8 shows 5ms RTT but loading google.com takes 8 seconds before the page appears. Trace the DNS resolution path to identify where the delay is and what specific diagnostic step confirms this hypothesis.
choice_a: The slow loading is caused by a slow WAN link — DNS is not involved in page load speed
choice_b: The 5ms ping to 8.8.8.8 tests IP-layer reachability but DNS resolution for google.com must query the internal DNS server first — if the internal DNS forwarder is slow; misconfigured; or experiencing a high query load the DNS lookup itself (not the connection) introduces the delay; page load: browser issues DNS query → DNS resolver times out or is slow → 8 second wait → eventually resolves → connection is fast (5ms ping) → page loads quickly; diagnostic confirmation: run nslookup google.com from command line and time the response — if it takes ~8 seconds the delay is in DNS resolution not in the connection; compare nslookup google.com [internal DNS] vs nslookup google.com 8.8.8.8
choice_c: The issue is MTU fragmentation — ICMP ping uses small packets; full-size HTTP packets fragment and drop
choice_d: The 8-second delay is caused by the browser's connection timeout — the web server is throttling connections
explanation: DNS resolution delay diagnosis: nslookup google.com → measures full resolution time including forwarder. nslookup google.com 8.8.8.8 → bypasses internal DNS; measures 8.8.8.8 directly. If internal is slow but direct is fast → problem is internal forwarder or internal DNS server performance. Check internal DNS server: excessive query queue; forwarder connectivity; or conditional forwarder misconfiguration routing external queries to wrong server. Fix: add faster forwarder; check DNS server CPU/memory; verify forwarder network path.
aspect_key: upstream resolver for unresolved/non-local queries

question: A network must be designed to carry 10 simultaneous HD video streams of 8 Mbps each plus 100 VoIP calls at 64 Kbps each plus management traffic of approximately 2 Mbps. Using multiplexing principles: calculate the minimum bandwidth required and explain why the actual provisioned bandwidth should exceed this calculation.
choice_a: The minimum bandwidth is 80 Mbps because only video streams count — VoIP and management are negligible
choice_b: Minimum calculation: video = 10 × 8 Mbps = 80 Mbps; VoIP = 100 × 64 Kbps = 6.4 Mbps; management = 2 Mbps; total minimum = 88.4 Mbps; the provisioned bandwidth should exceed this because: (1) statistical multiplexing assumes streams are not all at peak simultaneously but video streams are constant-bit-rate and always consume their full 8 Mbps each; (2) TCP congestion control overhead adds 10-20% for retransmissions and acknowledgments; (3) network utilization above 80% causes queuing delays that severely impact VoIP jitter; best practice is to provision at 110-120% of calculated peak: ~97-106 Mbps — a 100 Mbps or 1 Gbps link is required
choice_c: Multiplexing allows the total bandwidth to be halved because streams share the link efficiently
choice_d: Only the largest stream's bandwidth needs to be provisioned because multiplexing handles the rest
explanation: Design calculation: 80 + 6.4 + 2 = 88.4 Mbps minimum. Provisioning rule: provision to ≤70-80% utilization for QoS (VoIP needs consistent low-latency service). At 88.4 Mbps on a 100 Mbps link = 88.4% utilization — too high for VoIP quality. Required link: 100 Mbps insufficient (8.4% headroom). Recommend: 1 Gbps with QoS policies prioritizing RTP/VoIP. Alternatively: 100 Mbps with strict QoS policing video streams to 75 Mbps to protect VoIP.
aspect_key: multiple data streams share one medium

question: A Wireshark capture on a corporate laptop connected to a guest Wi-Fi network shows broadcast DHCP Discover packets from other devices on the segment. A network student claims this means any laptop on the network can capture all DHCP traffic including credentials being exchanged. Evaluate this claim — is the student correct; and what is the actual security boundary of DHCP on a shared network segment?
choice_a: The student is correct — all DHCP credentials including passwords are visible to any device sharing the segment
choice_b: The student is partially correct about visibility but incorrect about credentials — DHCP does not transmit passwords or user credentials at any point; it only exchanges IP configuration parameters (IP; mask; gateway; DNS) plus the client's MAC address; the risk is that an attacker can see which MAC addresses and hostnames are requesting leases (device fingerprinting) and can operate a rogue DHCP server to provide malicious gateway or DNS settings (on-path attack) — but no authentication credentials are exposed in DHCP itself; the real concern is ARP-based attacks or rogue DHCP rather than credential capture
choice_c: DHCP packets are encrypted and cannot be read by Wireshark even on a shared segment
choice_d: Broadcast packets are only visible to the intended recipient — other devices receive them but cannot decode them
explanation: DHCP security reality: Discover/Offer/Request/ACK are all broadcast or unicast on UDP 67/68. Contents: client MAC; requested IP; offered IP; DHCP options (gateway; DNS; lease time; domain name). No passwords. Risks on shared segment: (1) rogue DHCP server sends malicious gateway → all traffic routes through attacker (MITM). (2) DHCP starvation: attacker requests all IPs with fake MACs exhausting the pool. (3) Device enumeration: attacker learns all device MACs/hostnames. Mitigation: DHCP snooping; 802.1X port authentication.
aspect_key: discover; offer; request; acknowledge

question: An enterprise wants to implement split-horizon DNS where internal clients resolve server.corp.example.com to the server's private IP (10.0.0.50) while external clients resolve the same FQDN to the server's public IP (203.0.113.50). Describe the architecture required and why a single DNS zone cannot accomplish this.
